## Question
For integers \(n\ge 1\) and \(0\le m<n\), write \(H(n,m)^{\mathrm{ab}}\) for the abelianization of the group \(H(n,m)\). Which of the following statements is equivalent to \(H(n,m)^{\mathrm{ab}}\cong \mathbb{Z}^2\)?

## Choices
A. \(n\equiv 0\pmod 6\) and \(m\equiv 2\pmod n\).
B. \(n\equiv 0\pmod 6\) and \(m\equiv 2\pm \frac n3\pmod n\).
C. \(n\equiv 0\pmod 6\) and \(m=2\).
D. \(n\equiv 0\pmod 6\) and \(m\equiv 2\pmod{n/3}\).
E. \(n\equiv 0\pmod 6\) and \(m\equiv 2+\frac n2\pmod n\).

## Question
Consider the annuity risk model in which the capital reserve process \(X^u=(X_t^u)\) with initial capital \(u\ge 0\) satisfies
\[
X_t^u=u+\int_0^t \kappa X_s^u(a\,ds+\sigma\,dW_s)+\int_0^t r(1-\kappa)X_s^u\,ds-ct+\sum_{i=1}^{N_t}\xi_i,
\]
where \(W\) is a standard Wiener process, \(N\) is a Poisson process with intensity \(\lambda>0\), independent of \(W\), and \((\xi_i)_{i\ge1}\) are i.i.d. positive random variables, independent of \(N\), with distribution function \(F\) on \([0,\infty)\). Let
\[
\tau^u:=\inf\{t\ge 0:X_t^u\le 0\},\qquad \Phi(u):={\bf P}(\tau^u=\infty),\qquad \Psi(u):=1-\Phi(u).
\]
Assume \(F(0)=0\), \({\bf E}[\xi]<\infty\), \(\sigma>0\), and
\[
\gamma:=\frac{(a-r)\kappa+r}{\kappa^2\sigma^2}>1.
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the asymptotic behavior of the ruin probability \(\Psi(u)\) as \(u\to\infty\)?

## Choices
A. There exists a finite constant \(C>0\) such that
\[
\Psi(u)=O\!\left(u^{-\gamma+1}\right)\qquad (u\to\infty).
\]
B. There exists \(\varepsilon>0\) such that
\[
\Psi(u)=O\!\left(u^{-\gamma+1-\varepsilon}\right)\qquad (u\to\infty).
\]
C. There exists a finite constant \(C>0\) such that
\[
\Phi(u)=1-C\,u^{-\gamma}+o\!\left(u^{-\gamma}\right)
\qquad (u\to\infty).
\]
D. There exists a finite constant \(C>0\) such that
\[
\Psi(u)\sim C\,u^{-\gamma+1}\qquad (u\to\infty).
\]
E. There exists a finite constant \(C>0\) such that
\[
\Psi(u)\sim C\,u^{-\gamma}\qquad (u\to\infty).
\]

## Question
A countable discrete group $G$ is called a $W^*$-McDuff group if its group von Neumann algebra $L(G)$ is a McDuff II$_1$ factor. Here, $G$ is called McDuff superrigid in the strongest sense if whenever $L(G)\cong L(H)$ for some group $H$, one has $H\cong G\times A$ for some ICC amenable group $A$ (ICC means every nontrivial conjugacy class is infinite). What is the strongest existence statement that can be proved about countable $W^*$-McDuff groups?

## Choices
A. There exists a countably infinite family $\{G_i\}_{i\in I}$ of pairwise non-isomorphic countable $W^*$-McDuff groups such that every $G_i$ is McDuff superrigid.
B. There exists a family $\{G_i\}_{i\in I}$ of countable ICC groups with $|I|=2^{\aleph_0}$ such that $G_i\not\cong G_j$ whenever $i\ne j$, and every $G_i$ is McDuff superrigid.
C. There exists a family $\{G_i\}_{i\in I}$ of countable $W^*$-McDuff groups with $|I|=2^{\aleph_0}$ such that $G_i\not\cong G_j$ whenever $i\ne j$, and every $G_i$ is McDuff superrigid in the strongest sense.
D. There exists a family $\{G_i\}_{i\in I}$ of countable $W^*$-McDuff groups with $|I|=2^{\aleph_0}$ such that $G_i\not\cong G_j$ whenever $i\ne j$, and every $G_i$ is McDuff superrigid.
E. For every countable $W^*$-McDuff group $G$, there exists a family $\{G_i\}_{i\in I}$ of countable $W^*$-McDuff groups with $|I|=2^{\aleph_0}$ such that $G_i\not\cong G_j$ whenever $i\ne j$, every $G_i$ is McDuff superrigid, and $G$ is isomorphic to one of the $G_i$.

## Question
Let \(\Omega\subset \mathbb{R}^2\) be a bounded open set containing \(0\). Suppose \((v_n)\) is a sequence of solutions of
\[
-\Delta v_n=(\varepsilon_n^2+|x|^2)^{\alpha_n}V_n(x)e^{v_n}\qquad\text{in }\Omega,
\]
where \(\alpha_n\to \alpha_\infty\in(-1,1)\), and the coefficients \(V_n\) satisfy
\[
0<a\le V_n\le b<\infty,\qquad V_n\in C^0(\Omega),\qquad V_n\to V\ \text{locally uniformly in }\Omega.
\]
Fix any
\[
C_1>\max\left\{1,\frac{1+\alpha_\infty}{1-\alpha_\infty}\right\}
\]
and any compact set \(K\subset \Omega\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the uniform estimate satisfied by \((v_n)\) under these assumptions?

## Choices
A. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
B. There exists a constant \(C_2>0\), depending only on \(a\), \(b\), \(\operatorname{dist}(K,\partial\Omega)\), \(\alpha_\infty\), and the uniform modulus of continuity of \(V\) on \(K\), such that for every \(n\),
\[
\sup_K v_n + C_1\inf_{K} v_n\le C_2.
\]
C. There exists a constant \(C_2>0\), depending only on \(a\), \(b\), \(\operatorname{dist}(K,\partial\Omega)\), \(\alpha_\infty\), the uniform modulus of continuity of \(V\) on \(K\), and \(C_1\), such that for all sufficiently large \(n\),
\[
\sup_K v_n + C_1\inf_{\Omega} v_n\le C_2.
\]
D. There exists a constant \(C_2>0\), depending only on \(a\), \(b\), \(\operatorname{dist}(K,\partial\Omega)\), \(\alpha_\infty\), and the uniform modulus of continuity of \(V\) on \(K\), such that for every \(n\),
\[
\sup_K v_n + \frac{1+\alpha_\infty}{1-\alpha_\infty}\inf_{\Omega} v_n\le C_2.
\]
E. There exists a constant \(C_2>0\), depending only on \(a\), \(b\), \(\operatorname{dist}(K,\partial\Omega)\), \(\alpha_\infty\), and the uniform modulus of continuity of \(V\) on \(K\), such that for every \(n\),
\[
\sup_{\Omega} v_n + C_1\inf_{\Omega} v_n\le C_2.
\]

## Question
Let $h,r>0$ be fixed, and let
\[
X:=\{\rho\in AC([-h,h])\colon \rho(x)>0\text{ for all }x\in[-h,h],\ \rho(-h)=\rho(h)=r\}.
\]
For each $c\in\mathbb R$, define
\[
\mathcal F_c(\rho):=\int_{-h}^h\left(\rho(x)+\frac{c}{\rho(x)}\right)\sqrt{1+(\rho'(x))^2}\,dx,
\]
and let $\Pi_0(h,r)$ be a fixed quantity depending on $h$ and $r$. Which statement gives the existence, uniqueness, and case-by-case description of the minimizer of $\mathcal F_c$ in $X$?

## Choices
A. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
B. For every $c\in\mathbb R$, the functional $\mathcal F_c$ admits a unique minimizer $\rho_c\in X$. If $c<r^2$, then the equation \[\cosh\frac{2h}{E}=\frac{2r^2-E^2+2c}{E\sqrt{E^2-4c}}\] has exactly one positive solution $\smileacc{E}(c)$, and \[\rho_c(x)=\sqrt{\frac{\smileacc{E}}{2}\sqrt{\smileacc{E}^2-4c}\cosh\frac{2x}{\smileacc{E}}+\frac{\smileacc{E}^2}{2}-c}.\] In this case $\rho_c\in C^\infty([-h,h])$, $\rho_c$ is even and strictly convex, and $\rho_0<\rho_c<r$ on $(-h,h)$, where $\rho_0$ denotes the minimizer for $c=0$. If $c=r^2$, then $\rho_c(x)=r$ for all $x\in[-h,h]$. If $c>r^2$, then the equation \[\cosh\frac{2h}{E}=\frac{-2r^2+E^2-4c}{E\sqrt{E^2-4c}}\] has a unique positive solution $\frownacc{E}(c)$, and \[\rho_c(x)=\sqrt{-\frac{\frownacc{E}}{2}\sqrt{\frownacc{E}^2-4c}\cosh\frac{2x}{\frownacc{E}}+\frac{\frownacc{E}^2}{2}-c}.\] In this case $\rho_c\in C^\infty([-h,h])$, $\rho_c$ is even and strictly concave, and $r<\rho_c<\rho_\infty$ on $(-h,h)$, where $\rho_\infty(x)=\sqrt{h^2+r^2-x^2}$. Moreover, $\rho_c\to\rho_\infty$ uniformly on $[-h,h]$ as $c\to+\infty$. Finally, whenever $c_1<c_2$, one has $\rho_{c_1}<\rho_{c_2}$ on $(-h,h)$.
C. For every $c\in\mathbb R$, the functional $\mathcal F_c$ admits a unique minimizer $\rho_c\in X$. If $c<r^2$, then the equation \[\cosh\frac{2h}{E}=\frac{2r^2-E^2+2c}{E\sqrt{E^2-4c}}\] has exactly one solution $\smileacc{E}(c)>\Pi_0(h,r)$, and \[\rho_c(x)=\sqrt{\frac{\smileacc{E}}{2}\sqrt{\smileacc{E}^2-4c}\cosh\frac{2x}{\smileacc{E}}+\frac{\smileacc{E}^2}{2}-c}.\] In this case $\rho_c\in C^\infty([-h,h])$, $\rho_c$ is even and strictly convex, and $\rho_0<\rho_c<r$ on $(-h,h)$, where $\rho_0$ denotes the minimizer for $c=0$. If $c=r^2$, then $\rho_c(x)=r$ for all $x\in[-h,h]$. If $c>r^2$, then the equation \[\cosh\frac{2h}{E}=\frac{-2r^2+E^2-4c}{E\sqrt{E^2-4c}}\] has a unique positive solution $\frownacc{E}(c)$, and \[\rho_c(x)=\sqrt{-\frac{\frownacc{E}}{2}\sqrt{\frownacc{E}^2-4c}\cosh\frac{2x}{\frownacc{E}}+\frac{\frownacc{E}^2}{2}-c}.\] In this case $\rho_c\in C^\infty([-h,h])$, $\rho_c$ is even and strictly concave, and $r<\rho_c<\rho_\infty$ on $(-h,h)$, where $\rho_\infty(x)=\sqrt{h^2+r^2-x^2}$. Moreover, $\rho_c\to\rho_\infty$ pointwise on $[-h,h]$ as $c\to+\infty$, but the convergence is not uniform on $[-h,h]$. Finally, whenever $c_1<c_2$, one has $\rho_{c_1}<\rho_{c_2}$ on $(-h,h)$.
D. For every $c\in\mathbb R$, the functional $\mathcal F_c$ admits a unique minimizer $\rho_c\in X$. If $c<r^2$, then the equation \[\cosh\frac{2h}{E}=\frac{2r^2-E^2+2c}{E\sqrt{E^2-4c}}\] has exactly one solution $\smileacc{E}(c)>\Pi_0(h,r)$, and \[\rho_c(x)=\sqrt{\frac{\smileacc{E}}{2}\sqrt{\smileacc{E}^2-4c}\cosh\frac{2x}{\smileacc{E}}+\frac{\smileacc{E}^2}{2}-c}.\] In this case $\rho_c\in C^\infty([-h,h])$, $\rho_c$ is even and strictly convex, and $\rho_0<\rho_c<r$ on $(-h,h)$, where $\rho_0$ denotes the minimizer for $c=0$. If $c=r^2$, then $\rho_c(x)=r$ for all $x\in[-h,h]$. If $c>r^2$, then the equation \[\cosh\frac{2h}{E}=\frac{-2r^2+E^2-4c}{E\sqrt{E^2-4c}}\] has a unique positive solution $\frownacc{E}(c)$, and \[\rho_c(x)=\sqrt{-\frac{\frownacc{E}}{2}\sqrt{\frownacc{E}^2-4c}\cosh\frac{2x}{\frownacc{E}}+\frac{\frownacc{E}^2}{2}-c}.\] In this case $\rho_c\in C^\infty([-h,h])$, $\rho_c$ is even and strictly concave, and $r<\rho_c<\rho_\infty$ on $(-h,h)$, where $\rho_\infty(x)=\sqrt{h^2+r^2-x^2}$. Moreover, $\rho_c\to\rho_\infty$ uniformly on $[-h,h]$ as $c\to+\infty$. Finally, whenever $c_1<c_2$, one has $\rho_{c_1}>\rho_{c_2}$ on $(-h,h)$.
E. For every $c\in\mathbb R$, the functional $\mathcal F_c$ admits a unique minimizer $\rho_c\in X$. If $c=r^2$, then $\rho_c(x)=r$ for all $x\in[-h,h]$. If $c<r^2$, then $\rho_c\in C^\infty([-h,h])$, $\rho_c$ is even and strictly convex, and $\rho_0<\rho_c<r$ on $(-h,h)$. If $c>r^2$, then $\rho_c\in C^\infty([-h,h])$, $\rho_c$ is even and strictly concave, and $r<\rho_c<\rho_\infty$ on $(-h,h)$, where $\rho_\infty(x)=\sqrt{h^2+r^2-x^2}$. Moreover, $\rho_c\to\rho_\infty$ uniformly on $[-h,h]$ as $c\to+\infty$. Finally, whenever $c_1<c_2$, one has $\rho_{c_1}<\rho_{c_2}$ on $(-h,h)$.

## Question
Let \(F\) be a finite extension of \(\mathbb{Q}_p\), let \(D\) be a central division \(F\)-algebra of dimension \(d^2\), let \(m\ge 1\), and set \(G=\mathrm{GL}_m(D)\). Let \(n=md\), assume \(n\) is even, let \(E/F\) be a quadratic extension, and let \(H\) be the centralizer of \(E^\times\) in \(G\). A smooth representation \(\pi\) of \(G\) is called \(E\)-distinguished if \(\operatorname{Hom}_H(\pi,\mathbb{C})\neq 0\). Let \(\pi\) be a generic representation of \(G\), and let \(\phi=\phi_\pi\) be its \(L\)-parameter, viewed as a representation of the Weil-Deligne group \(WD_F\). Assume that \(\phi\) takes values in \(\mathrm{Sp}_n(\mathbb{C})\) and can be written as
\[
\phi = \bigoplus_{i\in I_\phi} \phi_i \oplus \bigoplus_{j\in J_\phi}(\phi_j\oplus\phi_j^\vee),
\]
where \(I_\phi\) and \(J_\phi\) are finite sets, each \(\phi_k\colon WD_F\to\mathrm{GL}_{n_k}(\mathbb{C})\) is a discrete \(L\)-parameter, for every \(i\in I_\phi\) the integer \(n_i\) is even and \(\phi_i\) takes values in \(\mathrm{Sp}_{n_i}(\mathbb{C})\), and \(\phi_i\not\simeq \phi_{i'}\) whenever \(i\neq i'\) in \(I_\phi\). Here \(\phi_j^\vee\) denotes the contragredient dual of \(\phi_j\). For such symplectic \(\phi_i\), write
\[
\varepsilon_E(\phi_i):=\eta_{E/F}(-1)^{n_i/2}\,\epsilon\bigl(1/2,\phi_i|_{WD_E},\psi_E\bigr),
\]
where \(\eta_{E/F}\) is the quadratic character of \(F^\times\) attached to \(E/F\), \(\psi_E=\psi\circ \operatorname{tr}_{E/F}\) for a nontrivial character \(\psi:F\to\mathbb{C}^\times\), and \(\epsilon\) is the Deligne-Langlands epsilon factor. Under these assumptions, which statement about the \(E\)-distinguished property of \(\pi\) is valid?

## Choices
A. \(\pi\) is \(E\)-distinguished if and only if \(\eta_{E/F}(-1)^{n/2}\,\epsilon\bigl(1/2,\phi|_{WD_E},\psi_E\bigr)=(-1)^m\).
B. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
C. If \(\pi\) is \(E\)-distinguished, then \(\varepsilon_E(\phi_i)=(-1)^{n_i/d}\) for every \(i\in I_\phi\).
D. \(\pi\) is \(E\)-distinguished if and only if \(\varepsilon_E(\phi_i)=(-1)^{n_i/d}\) for every \(i\in I_\phi\) such that \(n_i/d\) is odd; no further condition is required for the remaining indices in \(I_\phi\).
E. \(\pi\) is \(E\)-distinguished if and only if \(\varepsilon_E(\phi_i)=(-1)^m\) for every \(i\in I_\phi\).

## Question
Let \(\mathbb A\in\{\mathbb H,\mathbb O\}\), viewed as a real Euclidean space with its standard norm \(\|\cdot\|\), and let \(P_0(x)\in\mathbb R[x]\) be a central polynomial over \(\mathbb A\). Suppose its zero set \(V_0=Z(P_0)\) contains a non-real manifold of dimension \(d_{\rm M}>0\). Let \(P_\epsilon(x)\in\mathbb A[x]\) be a generically non-central analytic deformation of \(P_0\), meaning that the coefficients depend analytically on \(\epsilon\in\mathbb R\), \(P_\epsilon(x)=P_0(x)\) when \(\epsilon=0\), and for \(\epsilon\neq 0\) near \(0\) the automorphism stabilizer \(G_{P_\epsilon}=\{g\in\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb A): g(a_k)=a_k\text{ for all coefficients }a_k\}\) is trivial. Let \(V_\epsilon=Z(P_\epsilon)\). Define \(\mathcal{V}_\epsilon(x)=\|P_\epsilon(x)\|^2\) and consider the gradient flow \(\dot{x}=-\nabla \mathcal{V}_\epsilon(x)\). As \(\epsilon\to 0\), what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the collapse under this flow?

## Choices
A. The gradient flow realizes the collapse as a deformation retract, and the characteristic collapse time diverges as \(\epsilon\to 0\), without asserting the exact power law.
B. The gradient flow realizes the collapse as a deformation retract, and the collapse timescale exhibits critical slowing down with \(T_{\rm collapse}\propto \epsilon^{-2}\).
C. The gradient flow realizes the collapse as a deformation retract, and analyticity of the deformation already forces the Lojasiewicz exponent to be \(1/2\), so \(T_{\rm collapse}\propto \epsilon^{-2}\) for every analytic deformation, including non-generic ones.
D. The gradient flow realizes the collapse as a deformation retract, and for a generic non-central analytic deformation the characteristic collapse time scales as \(T_{\rm collapse}\propto \epsilon^{-1}\), reflecting a restoring rate that is linear in \(\epsilon\).
E. The gradient flow drives trajectories into the attractor set \(V_\epsilon\) with critical slowing down \(T_{\rm collapse}\propto \epsilon^{-2}\), but in general this gives only a retract of a neighborhood of \(V_0\), not a deformation retract of \(V_0\) onto \(V_\epsilon\).

## Question
Fix \(\varepsilon>0\). Let \(\theta\) satisfy \(2/15+\varepsilon<\theta<1-\delta_0\) for some absolute constant \(\delta_0>0\), and set \(h=X^{\theta}\). Let \(\mathbb P\) denote the set of prime numbers, and define
\[
\mathcal A_{\lambda}:=\left\{2^{\lfloor k_1^{r_1}\rfloor}+\cdots+2^{\lfloor k_{s-1}^{r_{s-1}}\rfloor}+2^{\lfloor \lfloor k_s^{\lambda}\rfloor^{r_s}\rfloor}:\ k_1,\dots,k_s\in\mathbb N\right\},
\]
with \(\mathcal A_{\lambda}(X):=\mathcal A_{\lambda}\cap[1,2X]\), and let
\[
\mathcal S:=\mathbb P+\mathcal A_{\lambda}(X).
\]
Write \(\#(\mathcal S\cap(x,x+h])\) for the number of elements of \(\mathcal S\) in the interval \((x,x+h]\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about \(\#(\mathcal S\cap(x,x+h])\) for integers \(x\in[X,2X]\) as \(X\to\infty\)?

## Choices
A. There exists \(c_{\varepsilon}>0\) such that, for all but \(O_{\varepsilon}\!\left(X\exp\!\left(-c_{\varepsilon}(\log X)^{1/3}\right)\right)\) integers \(x\in[X,2X]\), one has \(\#(\mathcal S\cap(x,x+h])\asymp h\); equivalently, the number of elements of \(\mathcal S\) in \((x,x+h]\) is bounded above and below by positive constant multiples of \(h\) outside an exceptional set of that size.
B. There exists \(c_{\varepsilon}>0\) such that, for all but \(O_{\varepsilon}\!\left(X\exp\!\left(-c_{\varepsilon}(\log X)^{1/4}\right)\right)\) integers \(x\in[X,2X]\), one has \(\#(\mathcal S\cap(x,x+h])\asymp h\), with implied constants absolute; in particular, they are uniform in \(\lambda\), \(\varepsilon\), and \(\theta\).
C. There exists \(c_{\varepsilon}>0\) such that, for all but \(O_{\varepsilon}\!\left(X\exp\!\left(-c_{\varepsilon}(\log X)^{1/4}\right)\right)\) integers \(x\in[X,2X]\), one has \(\#(\mathcal S\cap(x,x+h])\asymp h/\log X\); equivalently, the number of elements of \(\mathcal S\) in \((x,x+h]\) is bounded above and below by positive constant multiples of \(h/\log X\) outside that exceptional set.
D. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
E. There exists a set \(\mathcal E_X\subset [X,2X]\cap\mathbb Z\) with \(\#\mathcal E_X=o(X)\) such that for every integer \(x\in([X,2X]\cap\mathbb Z)\setminus \mathcal E_X\), one has \(\#(\mathcal S\cap(x,x+h])\asymp h\).

## Question
Let \(\Omega\subset \mathbb{R}^n\) be a locally \(C^{1,1}\) domain, let \(0<s<1\), and fix a boundary point of \(\partial\Omega\). For \(r>0\), let \(B_r\) denote the ball of radius \(r\) centered at that boundary point. Assume \(g\in L^\infty(\Omega\cap B_4)\), and let \(u\) be a nonnegative classical solution of
\[
\begin{cases}
(-\Delta)^s u=g & \text{in } \Omega\cap B_4,\\
u=0 & \text{in } B_4\setminus \Omega.
\end{cases}
\]
Here \(( -\Delta)^s\) denotes the fractional Laplacian
\[
(-\Delta)^s u(x)=C_{n,s}\,\mathrm{P.V.}\!\int_{\mathbb{R}^n}\frac{u(x)-u(y)}{|x-y|^{n+2s}}\,dy,
\]
and, in the classical sense, one assumes \(u\in C^{1,1}_{\mathrm{loc}}(\Omega)\cap \mathcal L_{2s}\), where
\[
\mathcal L_{2s}=\left\{u\in L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}(\mathbb{R}^n):\int_{\mathbb{R}^n}\frac{|u(x)|}{1+|x|^{n+2s}}\,dx<\infty\right\}.
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the quantitative boundary regularity estimate for \(u\) in \(\Omega\cap B_{1/2}\)?

## Choices
A. One has \(u\in C^s(\Omega\cap B_{1/2})\) and
\[
\|u\|_{C^s(\Omega\cap B_{1/2})}
\le C\bigl(\|g\|_{L^\infty(\Omega\cap B_4)}+\|u\|_{L^\infty(\Omega\cap B_4)}\bigr),
\]
where \(C\) depends only on \(n\) and the local \(C^{1,1}\) norm of \(\partial\Omega\), but is independent of \(s\).
B. One has \(u\in C^s(\Omega\cap B_{1/2})\) and
\[
\|u\|_{C^s(\Omega\cap B_{1/2})}
\le C\bigl(\|g\|_{L^\infty(\Omega\cap B_4)}+\|u\|_{L^\infty(\Omega\cap B_4)}\bigr),
\]
where \(C\) depends only on \(n\), \(s\), and the local \(C^{1,1}\) norm of \(\partial\Omega\).
C. One has \(u\in C^\alpha(\Omega\cap B_{1/2})\) for some \(\alpha\in(0,s]\), and in particular one may take \(\alpha=\min\{s,1-s\}\), with
\[
\|u\|_{C^\alpha(\Omega\cap B_{1/2})}
\le C\bigl(\|g\|_{L^\infty(\Omega\cap B_4)}+\|u\|_{L^\infty(\Omega\cap B_4)}\bigr),
\]
where \(C\) depends only on \(n\), \(s\), and the local \(C^{1,1}\) norm of \(\partial\Omega\).
D. One has \(u\in C^s(\Omega\cap B_{1/2})\) and
\[
\|u\|_{C^s(\Omega\cap B_{1/2})}
\le C\bigl(\|g\|_{L^\infty(\Omega\cap B_4)}+\|u\|_{L^\infty(\mathbb R^n)}\bigr),
\]
where \(C\) depends only on \(n\), \(s\), and the local \(C^{1,1}\) norm of \(\partial\Omega\)."
E. One has \(u\in C^{2s-\varepsilon}(\Omega\cap B_{1/2})\) for every \(0<\varepsilon<2s\), and
\[
\|u\|_{C^{2s-\varepsilon}(\Omega\cap B_{1/2})}
\le C_{\varepsilon}\bigl(\|g\|_{L^\infty(\Omega\cap B_4)}+\|u\|_{L^\infty(\Omega\cap B_4)}\bigr),
\]
where \(C_{\varepsilon}\) depends only on \(n\), \(s\), \(\varepsilon\), and the local \(C^{1,1}\) norm of \(\partial\Omega\).

## Question
In the continuum scale-free percolation model, let \(V\) be a unit-intensity homogeneous Poisson point process on \(\mathbb{R}^d\). Each \(x\in V\) carries an i.i.d. weight \(W_x\) with \(\mathbb{P}(W>w)=w^{-\beta}\) for \(w\ge 1\), and, conditional on \(V\) and the weights, distinct vertices \(x,y\) are connected independently with probability \(p_{xy}=1-\exp\{-\lambda W_xW_y/|x-y|^\alpha\}\), where \(|\cdot|\) is the Euclidean norm. Write \(E\) for the random edge set and \(x\leftrightarrow y\) for the event that \(x\) and \(y\) are joined by an edge. Define \(B_n=[-n,n]^d\), \(E_n=\{\{x,y\}\in E:x\in V\cap B_n\}\), \(e_n^*=\max_{\{x,y\}\in E_n}|x-y|\), and for fixed \(r>0\) let
\[
F(n,r)=\sum_{x\in V\cap B_n}\sum_{y\in V\cap B_n^c} \mathbf{1}_{\{|x-y|>r_n,\ x\leftrightarrow y\}} + \frac12\sum_{x\in V\cap B_n}\sum_{y\in V\cap B_n} \mathbf{1}_{\{|x-y|>r_n,\ x\leftrightarrow y\}},
\]
where \(r_n=c_n r\). Assume \(\min\{\alpha,\alpha\beta\}>d\) and either (a) \(0<\beta<1\) with \(\alpha\beta\in(d,2d]\) and \(c_n=C_1 n^{d/(\alpha\beta-d)}(\log n)^{1/(\alpha\beta-d)}\), or (b) \(\beta=1\) with \(\alpha\in(d,2d]\) and \(c_n=C_2 n^{d/(\alpha-d)}(\log n)^{2/(\alpha-d)}\), or (c) \(\beta>1\) with \(\alpha\in(d,2d)\) and \(c_n=C_3 n^{d/(\alpha-d)}\), where \(C_1,C_2,C_3>0\) are constants independent of \(n\). Writing \(\theta=\alpha\beta-d\) for \(0<\beta<1\) and \(\theta=\alpha-d\) for \(\beta\ge 1\), what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the asymptotic behavior of \(F(n,r)\) and \(c_n^{-1}e_n^*\) as \(n\to\infty\)?

## Choices
A. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
B. For every \(r>0\), \(F(n,r)\) converges in distribution to a Poisson random variable with mean \(\kappa r^{-\theta}\) for some finite constant \(\kappa=\kappa(d,\alpha,\beta,\lambda)\in(0,\infty)\), and \(\mathbb{P}(c_n^{-1}e_n^*\le r)\to \exp(-\kappa r^{-\theta})\); equivalently, \(c_n^{-1}e_n^*\) has a Fr\'echet limit with parameter \(\theta\) but with a non-unit scale.
C. For every \(r>0\), \(F(n,r)\) converges in distribution to a Poisson random variable with mean \(r^{-\theta}\), but because edges with both endpoints in \(B_n\) are counted with the factor \(1/2\), the event \(\{e_n^*\le r_n\}\) is not exactly the same as \(\{F(n,r)=0\}\); hence the Poisson limit for \(F(n,r)\) does not by itself yield the Fr\'echet limit for \(c_n^{-1}e_n^*\).
D. For every \(r>0\), \(F(n,r)\) converges in distribution to a Poisson random variable with mean \(r^{-\theta}\).
E. For every \(r>0\), \(F(n,r)\) converges in distribution to a Poisson random variable with mean \(r^{-\theta}\), and \(\mathbb{P}(c_n^{-1}e_n^*\le r)\to \exp(-r^{-\theta})\), so \(c_n^{-1}e_n^*\) converges in distribution to a Fr\'echet random variable with parameter \(\theta\); however, in the borderline regime \(\beta=1\) this conclusion requires \(\alpha\in(d,2d)\), excluding the endpoint \(\alpha=2d\).

## Question
In the spectral action matrix model, let $N\in\mathbb{N}$, let $\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_N$ be spectral parameters, and for any listed arguments let $f'[\mu_0,\dots,\mu_m]$ denote the divided difference of $f'$, defined inductively by $f'[x]:=f'(x)$ and $f'[x,y]:=\frac{f'(x)-f'(y)}{x-y}$ (and similarly for higher orders). For fixed external labels $i,j\in\{1,\dots,N\}$, consider the two ribbon-graph amplitudes with one vertex carrying external labels $i$ and $j$: (1) the amplitude with a single attached loop, and (2) the amplitude with two attached loops. Which statement holds for every such pair $(i,j)$?

## Choices
A. For every fixed $i,j\in\{1,\dots,N\}$, the single-loop amplitude equals
\[
\sum_{k=1}^N \frac{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_j,\lambda_i]}{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k]},
\]
and the two-loop amplitude equals
\[
\sum_{k,l=1}^N \frac{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_l,\lambda_l,\lambda_j,\lambda_i]}{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k]f'[\lambda_k,\lambda_l]}.
\]
So the denominator has the same two factors as in the standard two-loop pattern, but the higher divided difference repeats the internal label $l$ rather than $k$.
B. For every fixed $i,j\in\{1,\dots,N\}$, the single-loop amplitude is given by
\[
\sum_{k=1}^N \frac{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_j,\lambda_i]}{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k]},
\]
while the two-loop amplitude is a double sum over $k,l\in\{1,\dots,N\}$ whose numerator is
\[
f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_l,\lambda_k,\lambda_j,\lambda_i],
\]
and whose denominator is a product of two second-order divided differences.
C. For every fixed $i,j\in\{1,\dots,N\}$, the single-loop amplitude equals
\[
\sum_{k=1}^N \frac{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_j,\lambda_i]}{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k]},
\]
and the two-loop amplitude equals
\[
\sum_{k,l=1}^N \frac{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_l,\lambda_k,\lambda_j,\lambda_i]}{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_l]f'[\lambda_k,\lambda_l]}.
\]
In other words, the two denominator factors are attached to the pairs $(j,l)$ and $(k,l)$.
D. For every fixed $i,j\in\{1,\dots,N\}$, the single-loop amplitude equals
\[
\sum_{k=1}^N \frac{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_j,\lambda_i]}{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k]},
\]
and the two-loop amplitude equals
\[
\sum_{k,l=1}^N \frac{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k,\lambda_l,\lambda_k,\lambda_j,\lambda_i]}{f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_k]f'[\lambda_j,\lambda_l]}.
\]
Thus each added loop contributes one second-order divided-difference factor, both referenced to the external label $j$.
E. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

## Question
Let $(F_n)_{n\ge 0}$ be the Fibonacci numbers, defined by $F_0=0$, $F_1=1$, and $F_{n+2}=F_{n+1}+F_n$. Every integer $N\ge 1$ has a unique Zeckendorf representation
\[
N=\sum_{i=2}^k \varepsilon_i F_i,
\]
where $\varepsilon_i\in\{0,1\}$, $\varepsilon_k=1$, and $\varepsilon_i\varepsilon_{i+1}=0$. Write $\operatorname{rev}(Z(N))=\varepsilon_2\varepsilon_3\cdots \varepsilon_k$ for the Zeckendorf digits read from least significant to most significant, and let $\widetilde Z(N)=\operatorname{rev}(Z(N))\,\#^{\omega}$, where $\#\notin\{0,1\}$ is a padding symbol.

Let $u\ge 1$ and $q\ge 2$ be integers, let $\mathcal F$ be a finite family of non-empty binary words, let $L=\max\{|f|:f\in\mathcal F\}$, and fix $M\ge L$. Define
\[
\mathcal K_{\mathcal F}^{(M)}=\{N\ge 1:\text{ the prefix of length }M\text{ of }\widetilde Z(N)\text{ contains no factor from }\mathcal F\},
\]
and
\[
S_u^{(M)}=\{n\ge 0: u q^n\in \mathcal K_{\mathcal F}^{(M)}\}.
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about $S_u^{(M)}$?

## Choices
A. $S_u^{(M)}$ is either finite or ultimately periodic: there exist integers $n_0\ge 0$ and $p\ge 1$ such that
\[
n\in S_u^{(M)}\iff n+p\in S_u^{(M)}\qquad (n\ge n_0).
\]
Moreover, one can effectively decide whether $S_u^{(M)}$ is finite or infinite and, in the infinite case, compute an eventual period $p$; however, in general there is no procedure that always computes a valid preperiod $n_0$ or lists all elements when $S_u^{(M)}$ is finite.
B. $S_u^{(M)}$ is either finite or ultimately periodic: there exist integers $n_0\ge 0$ and $p\ge 1$ such that
\[
n\in S_u^{(M)}\iff n+p\in S_u^{(M)}\qquad (n\ge n_0).
\]
Moreover, one can effectively compute such a pair $(n_0,p)$ and decide whether $S_u^{(M)}$ is finite or infinite; in the finite case, the same procedure outputs all elements of $S_u^{(M)}$.
C. $S_u^{(M)}$ is ultimately periodic: there exist integers $n_0\ge 0$ and $p\ge 1$ such that
\[
n\in S_u^{(M)}\iff n+p\in S_u^{(M)}\qquad (n\ge n_0).
\]
Moreover, one can effectively compute such a pair $(n_0,p)$.
D. $S_u^{(M)}$ is either finite or ultimately periodic: there exist integers $n_0\ge 0$ and $p\ge 1$ such that
\[
n\in S_u^{(M)}\iff n+p\in S_u^{(M)}\qquad (n\ge n_0).
\]
Moreover, one can always choose these with $n_0<3^M$ and $1\le p\le 3^M$, and effectively compute such a pair together with a decision procedure for whether $S_u^{(M)}$ is finite or infinite; in the finite case, the same procedure outputs all elements of $S_u^{(M)}$.
E. $S_u^{(M)}$ is either finite or ultimately periodic: for each fixed $q$, $\mathcal F$, and $M$, there exist integers $n_0\ge 0$ and $p\ge 1$, independent of $u$, such that for every $u\ge 1$ one has
\[
n\in S_u^{(M)}\iff n+p\in S_u^{(M)}\qquad (n\ge n_0).
\]
Moreover, one can effectively compute such a universal pair $(n_0,p)$ and decide for each $u$ whether $S_u^{(M)}$ is finite or infinite; in the finite case, the same procedure outputs all elements of $S_u^{(M)}$.

What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the quantitative estimate for $\operatorname{vol}(M-\gamma)$, with a constant independent of $M$, $\gamma$, $\ell$, and $R$?

\]

\[

\]

\[

\]
Define the Hilbert space \(\mathcal H_R\) by
\[
\langle f,g\rangle_{\mathcal H_R}:=\int_{-\infty}^R f(x)\overline{g(x)}\,dx+\int_R^{\infty}\frac{1-\phi^2(x)}{1-\phi^2(R)}f(x)\overline{g(x)}\,dx,
\]
with norm \(\|f\|_{\mathcal H_R}=\langle f,f\rangle_{\mathcal H_R}^{1/2}\). For \(\psi,\phi\in \mathcal E\cap\mathcal H_R\), define
\[
\rho_R(\psi,\phi)=\|\psi_x-\phi_x\|_{L^2(\mathbb R)}^2+\|\psi-\phi\|_{\mathcal H_R}^2+\||\psi|^2-|\phi|^2\|_{L^2(R,\infty)}^2.
\]
Here \(\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb R\) represent phase and spatial shifts via \(e^{i\alpha}\psi(t,\cdot+\beta)\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about every sufficiently small perturbation \(\psi_0\in\mathcal E\) of \(\phi\) measured by \(\rho_R\)?

\[
i\psi_t=\psi_{xx}-\psi+4|\psi|^2\psi-3|\psi|^4\psi
\]
with initial condition \(\psi|_{t=0}=\psi_0\) satisfies
\[
\inf_{\beta\in\mathbb R}\rho_R\bigl(\psi(t,\cdot+\beta),\phi\bigr)\le \epsilon^2

Let \(X\) be a compact arithmetic congruence complex hyperbolic surface, and let \(\psi\) be an \(L^2\)-normalized Hecke--Maass form on \(X\) with spectral parameter \(\lambda>0\). Let \(\Pi\) denote the space of unit-length geodesic segments in \(X\), and for \(\gamma\in\Pi\) let \(\mathcal T_{\lambda^{-1/2}}(\gamma)\) be the \(\lambda^{-1/2}\)-neighborhood of \(\gamma)\). If the Kakeya--Nikodym norm is defined by
\[
\|\!\|\!\|\psi\|\!\|\!\|_{KN}:=\sup_{\gamma\in\Pi}\|\psi|_{\mathcal T_{\lambda^{-1/2}}(\gamma)}\|_2,
\]
what is the strongest statement that can be proved about \(\|\!\|\!\|\psi\|\!\|\!\|_{KN}\) for every \(\epsilon>0\)?

## Choices
A. There exists a constant \(C_X>0\) such that for every \(\epsilon>0\) and every \(\lambda>0\), one has \(\|\!\|\!\|\psi\|\!\|\!\|_{KN} \le C_X\lambda^{-1/28+\epsilon}\).
B. For every \(\epsilon>0\), one has \(\|\!\|\!\|\psi\|\!\|\!\|_{KN} \ll \lambda^{-1/28+\epsilon}\), where the implied constant depends only on \(X\) and \(\epsilon\).

E. There exists an absolute constant \(A>0\) such that \(\|\!\|\!\|\psi\|\!\|\!\|_{KN} \ll \lambda^{-1/28}(\log \lambda)^A\), where the implied constant depends only on \(X\).

$$\|\delta f\|\ge c\,\|f\|,$$
without needing the assumption
$$\|f\|\lesssim \frac{\beta^k}{(k+2)!}.$$

$$\|\delta f\|\ge c\,\|f\|,$$
with
$$c=\frac{\beta^k}{k!(k+2)^4},$$

$$\|\delta f\|\ge c\,\|f\|,$$

that is,

E. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

## Question
Let $k$ be a positive integer and let $\Gamma$ be a finite graph. Say that $\Gamma$ is $k$-set-homogeneous if, whenever two induced subgraphs of $\Gamma$ on $k$ vertices are isomorphic, some automorphism of $\Gamma$ maps the vertex set of one onto the vertex set of the other. Which statement is equivalent to $\Gamma$ being $k$-set-homogeneous?

## Choices
A. $\Gamma$ has the extension property only for automorphisms of a single $k$-vertex induced subgraph; that is, for every induced subgraph $\Delta$ of $\Gamma$ on $k$ vertices, every automorphism of $\Delta$ extends to an automorphism of $\Gamma$.
B. $\Gamma$ is $(k-1)$-set-homogeneous; that is, whenever two induced subgraphs of $\Gamma$ on at most $k-1$ vertices are isomorphic, some automorphism of $\Gamma$ maps the vertex set of one onto the vertex set of the other.
C. $\Gamma$ is $k$-homogeneous; that is, every isomorphism between induced subgraphs of $\Gamma$ on $k$ vertices extends to an automorphism of $\Gamma$.
D. $\Gamma$ is $k$-homogeneous with “induced” omitted; that is, every isomorphism between any pair of isomorphic subgraphs of $\Gamma$ on $k$ vertices, whether or not they are induced, extends to an automorphism of $\Gamma$.
E. $\Gamma$ is $(k-1)$-homogeneous, and whenever two induced subgraphs of $\Gamma$ on $k$ vertices are isomorphic, at least one isomorphism between them extends to an automorphism of $\Gamma$; that is, every isomorphism between induced subgraphs on at most $k-1$ vertices extends, and for subgraphs on $k$ vertices existence of one extending isomorphism suffices.

## Question
Let \(G\) be a countable recursively presented group (that is, a group given by a countable recursive presentation). Here \(\Gamma\) is required to be a finitely presented group; property \(\mathrm{(T)}\) means Kazhdan's property \(\mathrm{(T)}\); property \(\mathrm{(T_2)}\) means that \(\operatorname{H}^i(\Gamma;V)=0\) for every unitary \(\Gamma\)-representation \(V\) and \(i\in\{1,2\}\); and "Frobenius stable" means stable with respect to asymptotic homomorphisms into finite-dimensional unitary groups equipped with the Frobenius norm. Also, \(\operatorname{H}^2(\Gamma;V)\) denotes the second cohomology of \(\Gamma\) with coefficients in a unitary \(\Gamma\)-representation \(V\). ## Choices
A. There exists a finitely presented group \(\Gamma\) into which \(G\) embeds such that \(\Gamma\) has property \(\mathrm{(T)}\), is Frobenius stable, does not have property \(\mathrm{(T_2)}\), and nevertheless satisfies \(\operatorname{H}^2(\Gamma;V)=0\) for every finite-dimensional unitary \(\Gamma\)-representation \(V\).
B. There exists a finitely presented group \(\Gamma\) into which \(G\) embeds such that \(\Gamma\) has property \(\mathrm{(T)}\), is Frobenius stable, and does not have property \(\mathrm{(T_2)}\).

E. There exists a finitely presented group \(\Gamma\) into which \(G\) embeds such that \(\Gamma\) has property \(\mathrm{(T)}\), is Frobenius stable, does not have property \(\mathrm{(T_2)}\), and satisfies \(\operatorname{H}^2(\Gamma;V)\neq 0\) for every nontrivial unitary \(\Gamma\)-representation \(V\), but \(\operatorname{H}^2(\Gamma;V)=0\) for the trivial unitary representation.

## Question
Here, a hyperbolic lattice means an even integral lattice of signature $(1,\operatorname{rk} L-1)$, and the exceptional lattice of $L$ is the sublattice of vectors in $L$ with finite orbit under $\mathrm{O}^+(L)/W(L)$, where $\mathrm{O}^+(L)$ is the group of positive isometries of $L$ and $W(L)$ is the subgroup generated by reflections in $(-2)$-roots. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the exceptional lattice of every hyperbolic lattice $L$ of rank at least $46$?

## Choices
A. For every hyperbolic lattice $L$ of rank at least $46$, after passing to a maximal overlattice $L'\cong U\oplus E_8^n\oplus M$ with $n\ge 5$, the isometry $U\oplus E_8^3\cong U\oplus\Lambda$ with the Leech lattice $\Lambda$ already forces the exceptional lattice of $L$ to be trivial, without any further case-by-case analysis of $M$.
B. The exceptional lattice of every hyperbolic lattice $L$ of rank at least $46$ has rank at most $11$.
C. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
D. For every hyperbolic lattice $L$ of rank at least $46$, if $L'$ is a maximal overlattice of $L$ with $L'\cong U\oplus E_8^n\oplus M$ and $n\ge 5$, then the exceptional lattice of $L$ is exactly the root sublattice of $M$.
E. For every hyperbolic lattice $L$ of rank at least $46$, the lattice $L$ itself is isometric to $U\oplus E_8^n\oplus M$ for some $n\ge 5$ and some negative-definite lattice $M$ of rank at most $11$ whose root part contains no $E_8$ or ADE summand of rank at least $9$.

## Question
Consider an interval exchange transformation on $n$ subintervals whose parameter sequence $\{a_i,c_i\}$ is generated by a fixed Rauzy cycle $\gamma^{a,c}$ and satisfies
$$c_{i+1}=p a_i=p^2 c_i$$
for some integer $p\ge n+1$. For each step, let $\Theta_{c_i,a_i}$ be the corresponding Rauzy transition matrix, and write
$$\overline{\Theta}_{c_i,a_i}v:=\frac{\Theta_{c_i,a_i}v}{|\Theta_{c_i,a_i}v|},\qquad |w|:=\sum_k w_k,$$
with $e_j$ denoting the $j$th standard basis vector. Which description gives the complete collection of distinct invariant ergodic measures of this transformation?

## Choices
A. Exactly $\lfloor n/2\rfloor$ distinct invariant ergodic measures occur, namely
$$\lambda_j=\lim_{m\to+\infty}\overline{\Theta}_{c_m,a_m}\overline{\Theta}_{c_{m-1},a_{m-1}}\cdots\overline{\Theta}_{c_1,a_1}e_j,$$
for $j\in\{3,5,\dots,2\lfloor n/2\rfloor-1,n\}$.
B. Exactly $\lfloor n/2\rfloor$ distinct invariant ergodic measures occur, namely
$$\lambda_j=\lim_{m\to+\infty}\overline{\Theta}_{c_1,a_1}\overline{\Theta}_{c_2,a_2}\cdots\overline{\Theta}_{c_m,a_m}e_j,$$
for $j\in\{2,4,\dots,2\lfloor n/2\rfloor\}$.
C. There are at least $\lfloor n/2\rfloor$ distinct invariant ergodic measures, and for each
$$j\in\{3,5,\dots,2\lfloor n/2\rfloor-1,n\},$$
the limit
$$\lambda_j=\lim_{m\to+\infty}\overline{\Theta}_{c_1,a_1}\overline{\Theta}_{c_2,a_2}\cdots\overline{\Theta}_{c_m,a_m}e_j$$
exists and defines an invariant ergodic measure.
D. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
E. Exactly $\lfloor n/2\rfloor$ distinct invariant ergodic measures occur, namely
$$\lambda_j=\lim_{m\to+\infty}\overline{\Theta}_{a_1,c_1}\overline{\Theta}_{a_2,c_2}\cdots\overline{\Theta}_{a_m,c_m}e_j,$$
for $j\in\{3,5,\dots,2\lfloor n/2\rfloor-1,n\}$.

## Question
Let \(F_\tau\) be the matrix-valued process solving the Stratonovich SDE
\[
dF_\tau = F_\tau \circ dB_\tau, \qquad F_0=I,
\]
where \(B_\tau\) is a Brownian motion on \(\mathfrak{sl}(n):=\{A:\operatorname{tr}A=0\}\). Define the squared Frobenius norm by \(|F|^2:=\operatorname{tr}(F^*F)\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about \(\mathbb{E}|F_\tau|^{2p}\) for every integer \(p\ge 1\)?

## Choices
A. For every integer \(p\ge 1\), there exist constants \(c_{n,p},C_{n,p}>0\) such that
\[
 c_{n,p}\exp\!\left(\left(p+\frac{2p(p-1)}{n+2}\right)\tau\right)
 \le \mathbb{E}|F_\tau|^{2p}
 \le C_{n,p}\exp\!\left(\left(p+\frac{2p(p-1)}{n+2}\right)\tau\right)
\]
for all \(\tau\ge 0\).
B. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
C. For every integer \(p\ge 1\), the \(2p\)-th moment of the Frobenius norm satisfies
\[
 n\exp\!\left(\left(p+\frac{2p(p-1)}{n+2}\right)\tau\right)
 \le \mathbb{E}|F_\tau|^{2p}
 \le n^{p}\exp\!\left(\left(p+\frac{2p(p-1)}{n+3}\right)\tau\right).
\]
D. For every integer \(p\ge 1\), the \(2p\)-th moment of the Frobenius norm satisfies
\[
 n\exp\!\left(\left(p+\frac{2p(p-1)}{n+1}\right)\tau\right)
 \le \mathbb{E}|F_\tau|^{2p}
 \le n^{p}\exp\!\left(\left(p+\frac{2p(p-1)}{n+1}\right)\tau\right).
\]
E. For every integer \(p\ge 1\), the \(2p\)-th moment of the Frobenius norm is given exactly by
\[
 \mathbb{E}|F_\tau|^{2p}
 = n^{p}\exp\!\left(\left(p+\frac{2p(p-1)}{n+2}\right)\tau\right)
\]
for all \(\tau\ge 0\).

## Question
Let \(\mathbb S^n\) denote the \(n\)-dimensional sphere. For integers \(p,q\ge 1\), call an embedding \(f:M\hookrightarrow \mathbb S^N\) minimal if its image is a minimal submanifold of \(\mathbb S^N\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence of minimal embeddings of \(\mathbb S^p\times \mathbb S^q\times \mathbb S^1\) under these assumptions?

## Choices
A. For every pair of integers \(p,q\ge 1\), there exists a minimal embedding \(f:\mathbb S^p\times \mathbb S^q\times \mathbb S^1\hookrightarrow \mathbb S^{p+q+2}\) obtained by reflecting across an equatorial \(\mathbb S^{p+q+1}\) a smooth minimal hypersurface that meets that equator at a constant angle \(\theta\in(0,\tfrac{\pi}{2})\).
B. For every pair of integers \(p,q\ge 1\), there exists a minimal embedding \(f:\mathbb S^p\times \mathbb S^q\times \mathbb S^1\hookrightarrow \mathbb S^{p+q+1}\).
C. For every pair of integers \(p,q\ge 1\), there exists some integer \(N=N(p,q)\) and a minimal embedding \(f:\mathbb S^p\times \mathbb S^q\times \mathbb S^1\hookrightarrow \mathbb S^{N}\).
D. For every pair of integers \(p,q\ge 1\), there exists a minimal embedding \(f:\mathbb S^p\times \mathbb S^q\times \mathbb S^1\hookrightarrow \mathbb S^{p+q+2}\).
E. For every pair of integers \(p,q\ge 1\), there exists a minimal embedding \(f:\mathbb S^p\times \mathbb S^q\times \mathbb S^1\hookrightarrow \mathbb S^{p+q+2}\) whose image is contained in some totally geodesic equatorial \(\mathbb S^{p+q+1}\subset \mathbb S^{p+q+2}\).

## Question
Let \(G\) be a second-countable locally compact abelian group. Suppose \(f\in C_u(G)\) is mean almost periodic with respect to a Følner sequence \(\mathcal A\), or \(\mu\in \mathcal M^\infty(G)\) is a translation-bounded measure that is mean almost periodic with respect to a van Hove sequence \(\mathcal A\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence of an averaging sequence for which the same function or measure is Besicovitch almost periodic?

## Choices
A. There exists a translated subsequence \(\mathcal B=\{t_n+A_{k_n}\}\), determined solely by \(G\) and \(\mathcal A\), such that every function or measure that is mean almost periodic with respect to \(\mathcal A\) is Besicovitch almost periodic with respect to \(\mathcal B\).
B. There exists an untranslated subsequence \(\mathcal B=\{A_{k_n}\}\) of \(\mathcal A\) such that the same function or measure is Besicovitch almost periodic with respect to \(\mathcal B\).
C. There exists an averaging sequence \(\mathcal B\) such that the same function or measure is mean almost periodic with respect to \(\mathcal B\) (for instance, one may take \(\mathcal B=\mathcal A\)).
D. There exists another averaging sequence \(\mathcal{B}\) such that the same function or measure is Besicovitch almost periodic with respect to \(\mathcal{B}\).
E. There exists a single group element \(t\in G\) such that, while keeping the original averaging sequence \(\mathcal A\), the translate \(T_t f\) or the translated measure \(\delta_t*\mu\) is Besicovitch almost periodic with respect to \(\mathcal A\).

## Question
Let \(\Omega=B_R(0)\subset \mathbb{R}^n\) with \(n\ge 3\) and \(R>0\), and write \(B_r(0)=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^n:|x|<r\}\) for \(0<r<R\). Assume \(D,S\in C^3([0,\infty))\) satisfy \(D(s)>0\) on \([0,\infty)\), \(S(s)>0\) on \((0,\infty)\), \(S(0)=0\), and for some \(k_D,k_S>0\) and \(p,q\in\mathbb{R}\),
\[
D(s)\le k_D s^p,\qquad S(s)\ge k_S s^q\qquad (s\ge 1),
\]
with
\[
q-p>2-\frac n2,\qquad q>1-\frac n2.
\]
Consider the corresponding radially symmetric homogeneous Neumann problem on \(\Omega\), with positive radially symmetric initial data \(u_0,w_0\in W^{1,\infty}(\overline\Omega)\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about a uniform threshold conclusion for the initial data?

## Choices
A. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
B. There exist continuous functions \(M_1,M_2\in C^0([0,R])\) and a time \(T_*>0\) such that for every positive radially symmetric \(u_0,w_0\in W^{1,\infty}(\overline\Omega)\) satisfying
\[
\int_{B_r(0)} u_0\,dx\ge M_1(r),\qquad \int_{B_r(0)} w_0\,dx\ge M_2(r)\qquad (0<r<R),
\]
the corresponding problem admits a classical solution on \(\Omega\times(0,T_{\max})\), and in fact \(T_{\max}\le T_*\).
C. There exist constants \(m_1,m_2>0\) such that for every positive radially symmetric \(u_0,w_0\in W^{1,\infty}(\overline\Omega)\) satisfying
\[
\int_{\Omega} u_0\,dx\ge m_1,\qquad \int_{\Omega} w_0\,dx\ge m_2,
\]
the corresponding problem admits a classical solution on \(\Omega\times(0,T_{\max})\), and this solution blows up in finite time.
D. There exist continuous functions \(M_1,M_2\in C^0([0,R])\) such that for every positive radially symmetric \(u_0,w_0\in W^{1,\infty}(\overline\Omega)\) satisfying
\[
\int_{B_r(0)} u_0\,dx\ge M_1(r),\qquad \int_{B_r(0)} w_0\,dx\ge M_2(r)\qquad (0<r<R),
\]
the corresponding problem admits a classical solution on \(\Omega\times(0,T_{\max})\), where \(T_{\max}\in(0,\infty]\) denotes the maximal existence time.
E. There exist continuous functions \(M_1,M_2\in C^0([0,R])\) such that for every positive radially symmetric \(u_0,w_0\in W^{1,\infty}(\overline\Omega)\) satisfying
\[
\int_{B_r(0)} u_0\,dx\ge M_1(r)\qquad (0<r<R),
\]
and
\[
\int_{\Omega} w_0\,dx\ge M_2(R),
\]
the corresponding problem admits a classical solution on \(\Omega\times(0,T_{\max})\), and this solution blows up in finite time.

## Question
Let \(\Omega=G\times(-h,0)\) for some horizontal domain \(G\subset\mathbb R^2\), with horizontal variables \((x,y)\), vertical variable \(z\), and boundary parts \(\Gamma_u=G\times\{0\}\), \(\Gamma_b=G\times\{-h\}\), and \(\Gamma_l=\partial G\times(-h,0)\). Let \(u=(v,w)\) solve the three-dimensional primitive equations
\[
\partial_t v+u\cdot \nabla v-\Delta v+\nabla_H \pi=0,\qquad \partial_z\pi=0,\qquad \operatorname{div}u=0
\quad\text{in }\Omega\times(0,T),
\]
with initial condition
\[
v|_{t=0}=a \quad\text{in }\Omega.
\]
Here \(\nabla_H=(\partial_x,\partial_y)\), \(\operatorname{div}_H\) is the horizontal divergence, and the vertical average is
\[
\bar a(x,y):=\frac1h\int_{-h}^0 a(x,y,z)\,dz.
\]
Assume the initial data satisfy \(a\in H^1(\Omega)\), \(a_z:=\partial_z a\in L^3(\Omega)\), the compatibility condition \(\operatorname{div}_H\bar a=0\), and horizontal periodicity on \(\Gamma_l\). In the mixed boundary-condition case, also assume \(a=0\) on \(\Gamma_b\). Suppose moreover that \(u,\pi\) are periodic on \(\Gamma_l\times(0,T)\) and that either
\[
\partial_z v=0,\quad w=0 \quad\text{on }(\Gamma_u\cup\Gamma_b)\times(0,T),
\]
or
\[
\partial_z v=0,\quad w=0 \quad\text{on }\Gamma_u\times(0,T),
\qquad
v=0,\quad w=0 \quad\text{on }\Gamma_b\times(0,T).
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about \(v\) for every \(t\in[0,T]\) under these assumptions?

## Choices
A. It satisfies the uniform estimate
\[
\|v(t)\|_{H^1(\Omega)}^2+\int_0^t \|\nabla^2 v(s)\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2\,ds
\le B(a)e^{C\|a\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2}
\quad\forall t\in[0,T],
\]
where \(B(a)\) is a polynomial function of \(\|a\|_{H^1(\Omega)}\) and \(\|a_z\|_{L^3(\Omega)}\), and \(C>0\) depends only on \(\Omega\); however, this conclusion holds only in the fully Neumann case, while in the mixed boundary-condition case one only gets a time-dependent bound.
B. Under the stated assumptions one can conclude only the estimate
\[
\|v(t)\|_{H^1(\Omega)}
\le B(a,t)e^{C(\sqrt t+1)\|a\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2}
\quad\forall t\in[0,T],
\]
where \(B(a,t)\) is a polynomial function of \(\|a\|_{H^1(\Omega)}\), \(\|a\|_{L^8(\Omega)}\), \(\|a_z\|_{L^4(\Omega)}\), and \(\sqrt t\); in particular, no time-independent bound of the form stated in A is available from these assumptions.
C. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
D. It satisfies the bound
\[
\|v(t)\|_{H^1(\Omega)}^2
\le B(a)e^{C\|a\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2}
\quad\forall t\in[0,T],
\]
where \(B(a)\) is a polynomial function of \(\|a\|_{H^1(\Omega)}\) and \(\|a_z\|_{L^3(\Omega)}\), and \(C>0\) depends only on \(\Omega\).
E. It satisfies the a priori estimate
\[
\|v(t)\|_{H^1(\Omega)}^2+\int_0^t \|\nabla^2 v(s)\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2\,ds
\le B(a)e^{C\|a\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2}
\quad\forall t\in[0,T],
\]
but only after strengthening the hypothesis to \(a_z\in L^4(\Omega)\); under merely \(a_z\in L^3(\Omega)\), one has only the time-dependent proposition-type estimate.

## Question
Let $1<p<\infty$ and let $\Omega\subseteq\mathbb{R}^N$ be open. Assume that there exists $k\in\mathbb{N}\setminus\{0\}$ such that
\[
\lambda_{k,p}^{\rm LS}(\Omega)<\mathcal{E}_p(\Omega).
\]
Here $\lambda_{\ell,p}^{\rm LS}(\Omega)$ denotes the $\ell$-th Lusternik--Schnirelmann minmax value, and saying that a number $\lambda$ is an eigenvalue of the Dirichlet $p$-Laplacian on $\Omega$ means that there exists a nonzero $u\in W^{1,p}_0(\Omega)$ such that
\[
-\operatorname{div}\big(|\nabla u|^{p-2}\nabla u\big)=\lambda |u|^{p-2}u
\]
in the weak sense on $\Omega$. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the minmax values $\lambda_{\ell,p}^{\rm LS}(\Omega)$ under these assumptions?

## Choices
A. For each \(\ell\in\{1,\dots,k\}\), there exists a nonzero \(u_\ell\in W^{1,p}_0(\Omega)\) such that
\[
-\operatorname{div}\big(|\nabla u_\ell|^{p-2}\nabla u_\ell\big)=\lambda_{\ell,p}^{\rm LS}(\Omega)\,|u_\ell|^{p-2}u_\ell
\]
in the weak sense on \(\Omega\).
B. For every \(\ell\in\{1,\dots,k\}\), the value \(\lambda_{\ell,p}^{\rm LS}(\Omega)\) is an eigenvalue of the Dirichlet \(p\)-Laplacian on \(\Omega\). Moreover, if \(\mathcal E_p(\Omega)=+\infty\), then for every \(\ell\in\mathbb N\setminus\{0\}\) there exists a constant \(C_\ell=C_\ell(N,p,\Omega)>0\) such that every associated eigenfunction \(u_\ell\in W^{1,p}_0(\Omega)\) satisfies
\[
|u_\ell(x)|\le C_\ell\,\|u_\ell\|_{L^p(\Omega)}e^{-\alpha|x|}\qquad \text{for every }\alpha>0\text{ and every }x\in\Omega.
\]
C. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
D. If there exists \(k\in\mathbb N\setminus\{0\}\) such that \(\lambda_{k,p}^{\rm LS}(\Omega)\le \mathcal E_p(\Omega)\), then for every \(\ell\in\{1,\dots,k\}\) the minmax value \(\lambda_{\ell,p}^{\rm LS}(\Omega)\) is an eigenvalue of the Dirichlet \(p\)-Laplacian on \(\Omega\).
E. If there exists \(k\in\mathbb N\setminus\{0\}\) such that \(\lambda_{k,p}^{\rm LS}(\Omega)<\mathcal E_p(\Omega)\), then in fact every minmax value \(\lambda_{\ell,p}^{\rm LS}(\Omega)\), with \(\ell\in\mathbb N\setminus\{0\}\), is an eigenvalue of the Dirichlet \(p\)-Laplacian on \(\Omega\).

## Question
Let \(N\ge 1\), \(m\ge 2\), and assume the following: \(1<p<\frac{2^*}{2}\) if \(N\ge 3\), and \(p>1\) if \(N=1,2\), where \(2^*=\frac{2N}{N-2}\) for \(N\ge 3\); \(\mu_i>0\) for \(i=1,\dots,m\); \(\lambda_{ij}=\lambda_{ji}<0\) for all \(i\ne j\); and \(Q_\varepsilon(x-y_i):=Q(\varepsilon x-y_i)\), where \(Q\in C(\mathbb{R}^N)\) is nonnegative, has bounded support, and there exist exactly pairwise distinct points \(y_1,\dots,y_m\in\mathbb{R}^N\) such that \(Q(-y_i)=\|Q\|_\infty\) for all \(i=1,\dots,m\). For \(\varepsilon>0\), consider the coupled elliptic system
\[
\begin{cases}
-\Delta u_i + u_i
= \mu_i Q_\varepsilon(x-y_i) |u_i|^{2p-2}u_i
+ \displaystyle\sum_{j\neq i} \lambda_{ij} |u_j|^{p} |u_i|^{p-2}u_i, \\[4pt]
u_i \in H^1(\mathbb{R}^N),\quad i=1,\dots,m,
\end{cases}
\]
and write \(\mathbf u=(u_1,\dots,u_m)\in H\) with \(H=(H^1(\mathbb R^N))^m\). Let \(J_\varepsilon\) denote the associated energy functional. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence of sign-changing solutions for this system under these assumptions?

## Choices
A. For every \(\varepsilon>0\), the system admits infinitely many distinct sign-changing solutions \(\mathbf u\in H\).
B. For every \(\varepsilon>0\), the system admits a least-energy sign-changing solution \(\mathbf u_\varepsilon\in H\), and up to the natural sign symmetry \(\mathbf u\mapsto-\mathbf u\), this is the only sign-changing solution guaranteed by the theory.
C. There exists \(\varepsilon_0>0\) such that for every \(0<\varepsilon<\varepsilon_0\), the system admits a sequence of sign-changing solutions \(\mathbf{u}^{(k)}_\varepsilon\in H\) with
\[
0<J_\varepsilon\bigl(\mathbf{u}^{(1)}_\varepsilon\bigr)<\cdots<J_\varepsilon\bigl(\mathbf{u}^{(k)}_\varepsilon\bigr)<J_\varepsilon\bigl(\mathbf{u}^{(k+1)}_\varepsilon\bigr)<\cdots,
\]
and
\[
\lim_{k\to\infty}J_\varepsilon\bigl(\mathbf{u}^{(k)}_\varepsilon\bigr)=+\infty,
\]
but this multiplicity conclusion is available only for sufficiently small \(\varepsilon\), not for every fixed \(\varepsilon>0\).
D. For every \(\varepsilon>0\), the system admits a sequence of sign-changing solutions \(\mathbf{u}^{(k)}_\varepsilon=(u^{(k)}_{\varepsilon,1},\dots,u^{(k)}_{\varepsilon,m})\in H\), indexed by positive integers \(k\), such that
\[
0<J_\varepsilon\bigl(\mathbf{u}^{(1)}_\varepsilon\bigr)
<\cdots< J_\varepsilon\bigl(\mathbf{u}^{(k)}_\varepsilon\bigr)
< J_\varepsilon\bigl(\mathbf{u}^{(k+1)}_\varepsilon\bigr)
<\cdots,
\]
and
\[
\lim_{k\to\infty} J_\varepsilon\bigl(\mathbf{u}^{(k)}_\varepsilon\bigr)=+\infty.
\]
E. For every \(\varepsilon>0\), the system admits infinitely many sign-changing solutions \(\mathbf{u}^{(k)}_\varepsilon\in H\), but their energy levels remain bounded above for that fixed \(\varepsilon\); equivalently, there exists \(C_\varepsilon<\infty\) such that
\[
J_\varepsilon\bigl(\mathbf{u}^{(k)}_\varepsilon\bigr)\le C_\varepsilon\qquad\text{for all }k.
\]

## Question
Let n\ge 2, let \(\Delta=\partial_{x_1}^2+\cdots+\partial_{x_{n-1}}^2+\partial_y^2\), and let \(u=u(x,y,t)\) solve
\[
u_t=\Delta u+f(u), \qquad (x,y)\in \mathbb{R}^{n-1}\times\mathbb{R},\ t>0,
\]
with initial data \(u(x,y,0)=u_0(x,y)\). Assume the nonlinearity \(f\) satisfies
\[
f(0)=f(1)=0,\quad f'(0)>0,\quad f'(1)<0,\quad f(s)>0\ \text{for }s\in(0,1),\quad f(s)<0\ \text{for }s\in(-\infty,0)\cup(1,\infty).
\]
Let \(c=c_* > 2\sqrt{f'(0)}\), where \(c_*\) is the minimal speed of the one-dimensional traveling fronts, let \(\Phi_c\) be the corresponding one-dimensional traveling-wave profile, i.e.
\[
\Phi_c''+c\Phi_c'+f(\Phi_c)=0,\qquad \lim_{z\to-\infty}\Phi_c(z)=1,\quad \lim_{z\to\infty}\Phi_c(z)=0,
\]
and let \(\lambda_+\) be the larger root of \(\lambda^2+c\lambda+f'(0)=0\). Suppose also that
\[
\liminf_{y\to-\infty}\inf_{x\in\mathbb{R}^{n-1}} u_0(x,y)>0,
\]
and
\[
\limsup_{y\to\infty}\sup_{x\in\mathbb{R}^{n-1}} u_0(x,y)e^{-\lambda_1 y}<\infty
\quad\text{for some }\lambda_1<\lambda_+.
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the large-time asymptotic behavior of \(u\) and of the level set where \(u=\Phi_c(0)\)?

## Choices

## Question
Let $E$ be a complex holomorphic vector bundle on a compact complex manifold, and let $\alpha$ be a partition (Young diagram) of length at most $\operatorname{rk}(E)$. Write $\Schur^\alpha E$ for the associated Schur bundle and $\schur_\alpha$ for the corresponding Schur polynomial. For an integer $n\ge 0$, write $\nu\vdash n$ to mean that $\nu$ is a partition of $n$, and let $\ch_n$ denote the $n$th Chern character component. Which formula gives $\ch_n(\Schur^\alpha E)$ as a sum over partitions $\nu$ of $n$?

## Choices
A. \[\ch_n(\Schur^\alpha E)=\sum_{\nu\vdash n} \frac{\operatorname{rk}\!\big(\mathsf{D}^\nu(\schur_\alpha)\big)}{\ell(\nu)!}\,\ch^{(\nu)}(E).\]
B. \[\ch_n(\Schur^\alpha E)=\sum_{\nu\vdash n} \operatorname{rk}\!\big(\mathsf{D}^\nu(\schur_\alpha)\big)\,\ch^{(\nu)}(E^\vee).\]
C. \[\ch_n(\Schur^\alpha E)=\sum_{\nu\vdash n} c_\nu\,\ch^{(\nu)}(E)\qquad\text{for some rational coefficients }c_\nu\text{ depending on }\alpha.\]
D. \[\ch_n(\Schur^\alpha E)=\sum_{\substack{\nu\vdash n\\ \ell(\nu)\le \ell(\alpha)}} \operatorname{rk}\!\big(\mathsf{D}^\nu(\schur_\alpha)\big)\,\ch^{(\nu)}(E).\]
E. \[\ch_n(\Schur^\alpha E)=\sum_{\nu\vdash n} \operatorname{rk}\!\big(\mathsf{D}^\nu(\schur_\alpha)\big)\,\ch^{(\nu)}(E).\]

## Question
Let \(\theta\in(0,\pi/2]\) and \(1\le k\le n-1\). Assume \(f\in C^2(\mathbb C_\theta)\) is connected to \(1\) in the class \(\mathcal L_{-1/k}\), where \(\mathbb C_\theta\) is the capillary domain on which the \(k\)-th capillary area measure is prescribed. Here \(\overline{\mathbb R^{n+1}_+}=\{x\in\mathbb R^{n+1}:x_{n+1}\ge 0\}\), and a strictly convex capillary hypersurface \(\Sigma\) in \(\overline{\mathbb R^{n+1}_+}\) is one that, together with \(\partial\overline{\mathbb R^{n+1}_+}\), encloses a bounded closed region \(\widehat\Sigma\subset\overline{\mathbb R^{n+1}_+}\). A horizontal translation means a translation parallel to the boundary hyperplane \(\partial\mathbb R^{n+1}_+=\{x_{n+1}=0\}\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about existence and uniqueness for the prescribed \(k\)-th capillary area measure problem under these assumptions?

## Choices
A. For every \(\gamma\in(0,1)\), there exists a \(C^{3,\gamma}\) strictly convex capillary hypersurface \(\Sigma\) in \(\overline{\mathbb R^{n+1}_+}\) such that the associated body \(\widehat\Sigma\) satisfies the prescribed \(k\)-th capillary area measure problem. Moreover, \(\Sigma\) is unique up to horizontal translation in \(\overline{\mathbb R^{n+1}_+}\).
B. There exists a \(C^{3,\gamma}\) strictly convex capillary hypersurface \(\Sigma\) in \(\overline{\mathbb R^{n+1}_+}\), for some \(\gamma\in(0,1)\), such that the associated body \(\widehat\Sigma\) satisfies the prescribed \(k\)-th capillary area measure problem when \(\theta\in(0,\pi/2)\); however, the endpoint case \(\theta=\pi/2\) is not included in the existence theorem.
C. There exists a \(C^{3,\gamma}\) strictly convex capillary hypersurface \(\Sigma\) in \(\overline{\mathbb R^{n+1}_+}\), for some \(\gamma\in(0,1)\), such that the associated body \(\widehat\Sigma\) satisfies the prescribed \(k\)-th capillary area measure problem. Moreover, \(\Sigma\) is unique up to horizontal translation in \(\overline{\mathbb R^{n+1}_+}\).
D. There exists a \(C^{3,\gamma}\) strictly convex capillary hypersurface \(\Sigma\) in \(\overline{\mathbb R^{n+1}_+}\), for some \(\gamma\in(0,1)\), such that the associated body \(\widehat\Sigma\) satisfies the prescribed \(k\)-th capillary area measure problem. Moreover, any two such hypersurfaces differ by a rigid motion of \(\overline{\mathbb R^{n+1}_+}\) preserving the boundary hyperplane.
E. There exists a \(C^{3,\gamma}\) strictly convex capillary hypersurface \(\Sigma\) in \(\overline{\mathbb R^{n+1}_+}\), for some \(\gamma\in(0,1)\), such that the associated body \(\widehat\Sigma\) satisfies the prescribed \(k\)-th capillary area measure problem.

## Question
Let \(0<r<R\), let \(A=A(r,R)=\{z\in\mathbb{C}: r<|z|<R\}\), and define the Cauchy transform on the annulus by
\[
(C_A f)(z)=\frac{1}{\pi}\int_A \frac{f(w)}{z-w}\,dA(w),\qquad z\in A,\ f\in L^2(A),
\]
where \(dA\) denotes planar area measure. If \(J_\nu\) and \(Y_\nu\) denote the Bessel functions of the first and second kind, respectively, which statement about the \(L^2(A)\to L^2(A)\) operator norm of \(C_A\) holds?

## Choices
A. The norm is
\[
\|C_A\|_{L^2(A)\to L^2(A)}=\frac{2}{\kappa_1},
\]
where \(\kappa_1>0\) is the smallest positive solution of
\[
J_0(\kappa r)Y_0(\kappa R)-Y_0(\kappa r)J_0(\kappa R)=0.
\]
B. The norm is
\[
\|C_A\|_{L^2(A)\to L^2(A)}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{\lambda_1(A)}},
\]
where \(\lambda_1(A)\) is the first Dirichlet eigenvalue of \(-\Delta\) on \(A\); equivalently, \(\sqrt{\lambda_1(A)}\) is the smallest positive solution of
\[
J_0(\kappa r)Y_0(\kappa R)-J_0(\kappa R)Y_0(\kappa r)=0.
\]
C. The norm is
\[
\|C_A\|_{L^2(A)\to L^2(A)}=\frac{2}{\kappa_1},
\]
where \(\kappa_1>0\) is the smallest positive solution of
\[
J_0(\kappa r)Y_1(\kappa R)-Y_0(\kappa r)J_1(\kappa R)=0.
\]
D. The norm can be written in the form
\[
\|C_A\|_{L^2(A)\to L^2(A)}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{\mu_1}},
\]
where \(\mu_1\) is the first eigenvalue of a mixed Neumann--Dirichlet Laplacian problem on the annulus; equivalently, \(\mu_1=\kappa_1^2\) for a smallest positive root \(\kappa_1\) of a Bessel transcendental equation depending on \(r\) and \(R\).
E. The norm is
\[
\|C_A\|_{L^2(A)\to L^2(A)}=\frac{2}{\kappa_1},
\]
where \(\kappa_1>0\) is the smallest positive solution of
\[
J_1(\kappa r)Y_0(\kappa R)-Y_1(\kappa r)J_0(\kappa R)=0.
\]

## Question
Let $d\in\{1,2\}$, $\gamma>0$, and $\lambda,\omega\in\mathbb R$. Let $A$ be the self-adjoint operator associated with the Anderson Hamiltonian with spatial white noise $\xi$ in dimension $d$, let $\mu_0$ denote the lowest eigenvalue of $-A$, and let $\mathrm D(\sqrt{|A|})$ be the domain of $\sqrt{|A|}$. Consider the equation
\[
-A\phi-\lambda|\phi|^{2\gamma}\phi+\omega\phi=0
\]
for $\phi\in \mathrm D(\sqrt{|A|})$. Here, a positive solution means a solution with $\phi>0$, and a non-trivial non-negative solution means $\phi\ge 0$ and $\phi\not\equiv 0$. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the exact criterion for existence of positive solutions, together with the corresponding nonexistence of non-trivial non-negative solutions for the remaining parameter regimes?

## Choices
A. A positive solution $\phi\in \mathrm D(\sqrt{|A|})$ exists precisely in the following three cases: $\lambda>0$, $\gamma<\tfrac{2}{d}$, and $\omega>-\mu_0$; or $\lambda=0$ and $\omega=\mu_0$; or $\lambda<0$ and $\omega<-\mu_0$. For every other choice of parameters, there is no non-trivial non-negative solution in $\mathrm D(\sqrt{|A|})$.
B. Whenever one of the following three conditions holds, there exists a positive solution $\phi\in \mathrm D(\sqrt{|A|})$: $\lambda>0$ and $\omega>-\mu_0$; or $\lambda=0$ and $\omega=\mu_0$; or $\lambda<0$ and $\omega<-\mu_0$.
C. A positive solution $\phi\in \mathrm D(\sqrt{|A|})$ exists precisely in the following three cases: $\lambda>0$ and $\omega>-\mu_0$; or $\lambda=0$ and $\omega=\mu_0$; or $\lambda<0$ and $\omega\le -\mu_0$. For every other choice of $(\lambda,\omega)$, there is no non-trivial non-negative solution in $\mathrm D(\sqrt{|A|})$.
D. A positive solution $\phi\in \mathrm D(\sqrt{|A|})$ exists precisely in the following three cases: $\lambda>0$ and $\omega>-\mu_0$; or $\lambda=0$ and $\omega=-\mu_0$; or $\lambda<0$ and $\omega<-\mu_0$. For every other choice of $(\lambda,\omega)$, there is no non-trivial non-negative solution in $\mathrm D(\sqrt{|A|})$.
E. A positive solution $\phi\in \mathrm D(\sqrt{|A|})$ exists precisely in the following three cases: $\lambda>0$ and $\omega>-\mu_0$; or $\lambda=0$ and $\omega=\mu_0$; or $\lambda<0$ and $\omega<-\mu_0$. For every other choice of $(\lambda,\omega)$, there is no non-trivial non-negative solution in $\mathrm D(\sqrt{|A|})$.

## Question
Let \(\nu(\mathbb{CP}^n)\) denote the normal bundle of the complex projective space \(\mathbb{CP}^n\). For a partition \(\lambda=(\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_k)\) of \(n\), write \(t_\lambda=t_{\lambda_1}\cdots t_{\lambda_k}\), and let \(c^\nu_\lambda(\mathbb{CP}^n)\) be the Chern number of \(\nu(\mathbb{CP}^n)\) corresponding to the monomial symmetric function \(m_\lambda\). Define the generating function
\[
C^\nu(\mathbb{CP}^n,t):=\sum_{|\lambda|=n} c^\nu_\lambda(\mathbb{CP}^n)\, t_\lambda,
\]
where \(t=(t_1,\dots,t_n)\). If \(L_n(t_1,\dots,t_n)\) denotes the \(n\)-th Lagrange inversion polynomial, what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the identity satisfied by \(C^\nu(\mathbb{CP}^n,t)\)?

## Choices
A. \(C^\nu(\mathbb{CP}^n,t)=(-1)^n(n+1)L_n(t_1,\dots,t_n)\).
B. \(C^\nu(\mathbb{CP}^n,t)\) is a scalar multiple of the Lagrange inversion polynomial \(L_n(t_1,\dots,t_n)\).
C. \(C^\nu(\mathbb{CP}^n,t)=(n+1)L_n(t_1,\dots,t_n)\).
D. \(C^\nu(\mathbb{CP}^n,t)=(n+1)L_n(t_1,-t_2,t_3,-t_4,\dots,(-1)^{n-1}t_n)\).
E. \(C^\nu(\mathbb{CP}^n,t)=(n+1)L_n\!\left(\dfrac{t_1}{2!},\dfrac{t_2}{3!},\dots,\dfrac{t_n}{(n+1)!}\right)\).

## Question
Fix a depth \(L \ge 1\), input dimension \(d_{\mathrm{in}}\), output dimension \(d_{\mathrm{out}} \ge 1\), and Lipschitz activation functions \(\sigma^{(1)}, \ldots, \sigma^{(L)}\). For each \(n\), let \(f_{(n)}^{(L)} : \mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{in}}} \to \mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{out}}}\) be the output of a fully connected feedforward neural network with hidden-layer widths \(n_\ell = n_\ell(n)\), where \(n_0 := d_{\mathrm{in}}\), satisfying \(\lim_{n\to\infty} n_\ell / n \in (0,\infty]\) for every \(\ell = 1, \ldots, L-1\), and with i.i.d. Gaussian weights \(W_{i,j}^{(\ell)}\) such that \(\mathbb{E}[(W_{i,j}^{(\ell)})^2] = 1 / n_{\ell-1}\) for every layer \(\ell\) and every \(i,j\) (LeCun initialization). For a compact input set \(X \subset \mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{in}}}\), consider the rescaled random function \(x \mapsto (1/\sqrt{n}) f_{(n)}^{(L)}(x)\) as an element of \(C(X, \mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{out}}})\) equipped with the topology of uniform convergence. If, for each finite subset \(X_0 \subset X\), \(\pi_{X_0}(z)\) denotes the restriction of \(z \in C(X, \mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{out}}})\) to \(X_0\), and \(I_{X_0,\mathrm{out}}^{(L)}\) is the finite-dimensional rate function for the projected process on \((\mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{out}}})^{X_0}\), what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the asymptotic behavior, as \(n \to \infty\), of the laws of \(x \mapsto (1/\sqrt{n}) f_{(n)}^{(L)}(x)\) on \(C(X,\mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{out}}})\)?

## Choices
A. For every compact X \subset \mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{in}}}, the laws of the random functions x \mapsto (1/\sqrt{n}) f_{(n)}^{(L)}(x) satisfy a large deviation principle on C(X,\mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{out}}}) with speed n and rate function
\[
I_{X,\mathrm{out}}^{(L)}(z)=\sup_{\substack{X_0\subset X\ \mathrm{finite}\\ |X_0|\le 2}} I_{X_0,\mathrm{out}}^{(L)}\bigl(\pi_{X_0}(z)\bigr).
\]
B. For every compact X ⊂ R^(d_in), the laws of the random functions x ↦ (1/√n) f_(n)^(L)(x) satisfy a large deviation principle on C(X, R^(d_out)) with speed n and rate function
I_{X,out}^(L)(z) = sup_{X_0 ⊂ X finite} I_{X_0,out}^(L)(π_{X_0}(z)).
C. For every compact X \subset \mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{in}}}, the laws of the random functions x \mapsto (1/\sqrt{n}) f_{(n)}^{(L)}(x) satisfy a large deviation principle on C(X,\mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{out}}}) with speed n and rate function
\[
I_{X,\mathrm{out}}^{(L)}(z)=\max_{X_0\subset X\ \mathrm{finite}} I_{X_0,\mathrm{out}}^{(L)}\bigl(\pi_{X_0}(z)\bigr).
\]
D. For every compact X \subset \mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{in}}}, the laws of the random functions x \mapsto (1/\sqrt{n}) f_{(n)}^{(L)}(x) are exponentially tight on C(X,\mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{out}}}) with speed n.
E. For every compact X \subset \mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{in}}}, once the projected processes \(\pi_{X_0}((1/\sqrt{n})f_{(n)}^{(L)})\) satisfy LDPs for all finite \(X_0\subset X\), it follows automatically that the laws of x \mapsto (1/\sqrt{n}) f_{(n)}^{(L)}(x) satisfy a full large deviation principle on C(X,\mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{out}}}) with speed n and rate function
\[
I_{X,\mathrm{out}}^{(L)}(z)=\sup_{X_0\subset X\ \mathrm{finite}} I_{X_0,\mathrm{out}}^{(L)}\bigl(\pi_{X_0}(z)\bigr),
\]
without any separate exponential tightness argument.

## Question
Let $u_0\ge 0$ be an initial probability density on $\mathbb{R}^2$ such that $u_0\log u_0\in L^1(\mathbb{R}^2)$, $\nabla\log u_0\in W^{1,q}(\mathbb{R}^2)$ for some $q>2$, and $u_0\in L^1(\mathbb{R}^2,|x|^2dx)\cap L^p(\mathbb{R}^2)$ for $1\le p\le \infty$. Assume also that $\chi<4/c_*$, where $c_*$ is the optimal constant in the Gagliardo--Nirenberg inequality $\|\omega\|_{L^4(\mathbb{R}^2)}^4\le c_*\|\omega\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^2)}^2\|\nabla\omega\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^2)}^2$. For $T>0$, let $u^\varepsilon$ be the corresponding weak solution on $\mathbb{R}^2\times(0,T)$. Let $\Phi^\varepsilon$ denote the cutoff interaction kernel, and write spatial convolution as
\[
(\Phi^\varepsilon*u^\varepsilon)(x,t)=\int_{\mathbb{R}^2}\Phi^\varepsilon(x-y)u^\varepsilon(y,t)\,dy.
\]
For $1\le i\le N$, let $X_{N,i}^\varepsilon$ and $\overline X_i^\varepsilon$ solve, with the same $\mathbb{R}^2$-valued Brownian motions $B_i$ and the same initial data $\zeta_i$,
\[
dX_{N,i}^\varepsilon(t)=\Big(2\exp\Big(-\frac1N\sum_{j=1}^N\Phi^\varepsilon(X_{N,i}^\varepsilon(t)-X_{N,j}^\varepsilon(t))\Big)+2\Big)^{1/2}dB_i(t),\qquad X_{N,i}^\varepsilon(0)=\zeta_i,
\]
and
\[
d\overline X_i^\varepsilon(t)=\big(2\exp(-\Phi^\varepsilon*u^\varepsilon(\overline X_i^\varepsilon,t))+2\big)^{1/2}dB_i(t),\qquad \overline X_i^\varepsilon(0)=\zeta_i.
\]
Fix $m,N\in\mathbb N^+$ and parameters $0<\theta<\tfrac12$, $0<\alpha<\tfrac\theta2$, and $m>\tfrac{\theta+1}{1-2\theta}$. Under the cutoff scaling $\varepsilon\sim N^{-\gamma}$, what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the quantitative discrepancy between the particle system and the mean-field system?

## Choices
A. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
B. There exist $C(m,T)>0$ and parameters $\gamma,\eta>0$ such that
\[
0<\gamma<\min\Big\{\frac{\alpha}{3},\frac{-2\alpha+m(1-2\theta)-1}{4m+4}\Big\},
\]
and
\[
0<\eta\le \min\Big\{\theta-2\alpha,\,-(4m+4)\gamma-2\alpha+m(1-2\theta)-1\Big\},
\]
for which, for every $t\in[0,T]$,
\[
\mathbb P\Big(\sup_{0\le s\le t}\max_{1\le i\le N}\big|X_{N,i}^\varepsilon(s)-\overline X_i^\varepsilon(s)\big|>N^{-\alpha}\Big)\le C(m,T)N^{-\eta}.
\]
C. There exist $C(m,T)>0$ and exponents $\gamma,\eta>0$ such that, whenever the cutoff satisfies $\varepsilon\sim N^{-\gamma}$, one has for every $t\in[0,T]$,
\[
\mathbb P\Big(\max_{1\le i\le N}\big|X_{N,i}^\varepsilon(t)-\overline X_i^\varepsilon(t)\big|>N^{-\alpha}\Big)\le C(m,T)N^{-\eta}.
\]
D. Assume the cutoff parameter is chosen at the boundary value
\[
\gamma=\min\Big\{\frac{\alpha}{3},\frac{-2\alpha+m(1-2\theta)-1}{4m+4}\Big\},\qquad \varepsilon\sim N^{-\gamma}.
\]
Then there exist $C(m,T)>0$ and $\eta>0$ satisfying
\[
0<\eta\le \min\Big\{\theta-2\alpha,\,-(4m+4)\gamma-2\alpha+m(1-2\theta)-1\Big\},
\]
such that, for every $t\in[0,T]$,
\[
\mathbb P\Big(\max_{1\le i\le N}\big|X_{N,i}^\varepsilon(t)-\overline X_i^\varepsilon(t)\big|>N^{-\alpha}\Big)\le C(m,T)N^{-\eta}.
\]
E. For every $\gamma$ satisfying
\[
0<\gamma<\min\Big\{\frac{\alpha}{3},\frac{-2\alpha+m(1-2\theta)-1}{4m+4}\Big\},

there exists $C(m,T)>0$ such that, whenever $\varepsilon\sim N^{-\gamma}$, one has for every $t\in[0,T]$,

\mathbb P\Big(\max_{1\le i\le N}\big|X_{N,i}^\varepsilon(t)-\overline X_i^\varepsilon(t)\big|>N^{-\alpha}\Big)\le C(m,T)N^{-(\theta-2\alpha)}.
\]

## Question
Let \(g \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}\) and \(n \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}\) satisfy \(2g-2+n>0\). For an \(n\)-tuple \(\mathbf e=(e_1,\dots,e_n)\) of nonnegative integers with \(e_1+\cdots+e_n=3g-3+n\), define \(D(\mathbf e)=\langle \tau_{e_1}\cdots \tau_{e_n}\rangle_g\). Call \(\mathbf e\) concentrated if it is a permutation of \((3g-3+n,0,\ldots,0)\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence of a vector \(\mathbf e\) at which \(D\) attains its minimum?

## Choices
A. There exists a concentrated vector \(\mathbf e\) at which \(D\) attains its minimum, and this minimum value is \(\langle \tau_{3g-3+n}\,\tau_0^{n-1}\rangle_g=\frac{(n-1)!}{24^g\,g!}\), where \(\tau_0^{n-1}\) denotes \(n-1\) copies of \(\tau_0\).
B. There exists a concentrated vector \(\mathbf e\) at which \(D\) attains its minimum.
C. There exists a unique concentrated vector \(\mathbf e\) at which \(D\) attains its minimum, and every other valid exponent vector \(\mathbf e'\neq \mathbf e\) satisfies \(D(\mathbf e')>D(\mathbf e)\).
D. There exists a balanced vector \(\mathbf e\) at which \(D\) attains its maximum, and this extremal value is \(\langle \tau_{3g-3+n}\,\tau_0^{n-1}\rangle_g=\frac{1}{24^g\,g!}\), where \(\tau_0^{n-1}\) denotes \(n-1\) copies of \(\tau_0\).
E. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

## Question
Let \(d_1,d_2,d_3>1\) be positive integers such that any repeated value among \(d_1,d_2,d_3\) is odd. Let
\[
R=F_{\mathbb{Q}}[x_1,x_2,x_3]/(x_1^2,x_2^2,x_3^2),\qquad |x_i|=d_i,
\]
where \(F_{\mathbb{Q}}[x_1,x_2,x_3]\) denotes the free graded-commutative \(\mathbb{Q}\)-algebra on homogeneous generators \(x_1,x_2,x_3\). Thus \(R\) is the cohomology algebra of a product of three positive-dimensional spheres over \(\mathbb{Q}\). If \(A\) is an order in \(R\), meaning that \(A\) is a graded subring of \(R\) whose underlying additive group is a graded lattice in \(R\) (equivalently, it is generated by a homogeneous basis of the \(\mathbb{Q}\)-vector space \(R\)), what is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence of a CW-complex realizing \(A\) in integral cohomology?

## Choices
A. There exists a CW-complex \(X_A\) such that its integral cohomology ring embeds as a graded subring of \(A\); equivalently, \(H^*(X_A;\mathbb{Z})\cong B\) for some graded subring \(B\subseteq A\).
B. There exists a single CW-complex \(X\), depending only on the degree list \((d_1,d_2,d_3)\) and not on the choice of order \(A\), such that for every order \(A\) in \(R\) one has \(H^*(X;\mathbb{Z})\cong A\).
C. There exists a CW-complex \(X_A\) whose integral cohomology ring is isomorphic to \(A\) after quotienting by torsion; that is, \(H^*(X_A;\mathbb{Z})/\mathrm{tors}\cong A\).
D. There exists a CW-complex \(X_A\) whose integral cohomology ring is isomorphic to \(A\), provided that the degrees \(d_1,d_2,d_3\) are pairwise distinct.
E. There exists a CW-complex \(X_A\) whose integral cohomology ring is isomorphic to \(A\); that is, \(H^*(X_A;\mathbb{Z})\cong A\).

## Question
Let \(n\ge 2\) and \(0<s<1\). Let \(\mathbb H^n\) denote the \(n\)-dimensional hyperbolic space, and let \(( -\Delta_{\mathbb H^n})^s\) be the fractional Laplacian on \(\mathbb H^n\). Suppose \(u\in L^q(\mathbb H^n)\) for some \(q>p\) is a nonnegative weak solution of
\[
(-\Delta_{\mathbb H^n})^s u = u^p \quad \text{in } \mathbb H^n,
\]
meaning that for every \(\varphi\in C_c^\infty(\mathbb H^n)\),
\[
\int_{\mathbb H^n} u(x)(-\Delta_{\mathbb H^n})^s\varphi(x)\,dV_x
=\int_{\mathbb H^n} u(x)^p\varphi(x)\,dV_x.
\]
Here, saying that \(u\) is radially symmetric and nondecreasing about a point \(x_0\in\mathbb H^n\) means that \(u(x)\) depends only on the hyperbolic distance \(d_{\mathbb H^n}(x,x_0)\) and is nondecreasing as that distance increases. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about such solutions in the subcritical and critical cases \(1<p\le \frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\), assuming in addition that \(u\in L^{p+1}(\mathbb H^n)\) when \(1<p<\frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\)?

## Choices
A. If \(1<p<\frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\) and \(u\in L^{p+1}(\mathbb H^n)\), then \(u\equiv 0\). If \(p=\frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\), then there exists \(x_0\in\mathbb H^n\) such that \(u\) is radially symmetric about \(x_0\).
B. If \(1<p<\frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\) and \(u\in L^{p+1}(\mathbb H^n)\), then \(u\equiv 0\). If \(p=\frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\), then there exists \(x_0\in\mathbb H^n\) such that \(u\) is radially symmetric and nondecreasing about \(x_0\).
C. If \(1<p<\frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\) and \(u\in L^{p+1}(\mathbb H^n)\), then \(u\equiv 0\). If \(p=\frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\), then there exists \(x_0\in\mathbb H^n\) such that \(u\) is radially symmetric, nondecreasing about \(x_0\), and strictly positive on \(\mathbb H^n\).
D. If \(1<p<\frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\) and \(u\in L^{p+1}(\mathbb H^n)\), then \(u\equiv 0\). If \(p=\frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\), then there exists \(x_0\in\mathbb H^n\) such that \(u\) is radially symmetric and strictly increasing about \(x_0\).
E. If \(1<p<\frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\) and \(u\in L^{p+1}(\mathbb H^n)\), then \(u\) is strictly positive and radially symmetric about some \(x_0\in\mathbb H^n\). If \(p=\frac{n+2s}{n-2s}\), then there exists \(x_0\in\mathbb H^n\) such that \(u\) is radially symmetric and nondecreasing about \(x_0\).

## Question
Let \(u=u(t,x)\) solve the Ohta--Kawasaki equation on \((0,\infty)\times(0,L)\) with \(L=2\pi\),
\[
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t}=-\dfrac{1}{\gamma^2}\dfrac{\partial^4 u}{\partial x^4}-\dfrac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}(u-u^3)-\sigma(u-m), & (t,x)\in(0,\infty)\times(0,L), \\
\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}(t,0)=\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}(t,L)=\dfrac{\partial^3 u}{\partial x^3}(t,0)=\dfrac{\partial^3 u}{\partial x^3}(t,L)=0, & t\ge 0, \\
 u(0,x)=u_{\mathrm{in}}(x), & x\in[0,L],
\end{cases}
\]
where \(\gamma=\sqrt{8}\), \(\sigma=1/5\), \(m=1/10\), and
\[
 u_{\mathrm{in}}(x)=m+\frac{2}{10}\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi x}{L}\right)+\frac{2}{10}\cos\!\left(\frac{4\pi x}{L}\right).
\]
Let \(\bar u(t,x)\) be the specified approximate solution, described in terms of Chebyshev\(\times\)Fourier coefficients, given on \([0,30]\times[0,L]\) and extended to all \(t\ge 30\) by \(\bar u(t,x)=\bar u(30,x)\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the error between the exact solution \(u\) and this approximate solution \(\bar u\)?

## Choices
A. \[\sup_{t\in[0,\infty)}\sup_{x\in[0,L]}\big|u(t,x)-\bar u(t,x)\big|\le 4\times 10^{-8}.\]
B. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
C. \[\sup_{x\in[0,L]}\big|u(30,x)-\bar u(30,x)\big|\le 9\times 10^{-7}.\]
D. \[\sup_{t\in[0,\infty)}\left\|u(t,\cdot)-\bar u(t,\cdot)\right\|_{L^2(0,L)}\le 9\times 10^{-7}.\]
E. \[\sup_{t\in[0,\infty)}\sup_{x\in[0,L]}\big|u(t,x)-\bar u(30,x)\big|\le 9\times 10^{-7}.\]

## Question
Let \(L\) be a link in \(S^3\) that is non-split (it cannot be separated into two nonempty parts by a 2-sphere), nontrivial (not an unlink), and not the Hopf link. Let \(c(L)\) denote the crossing number of \(L\), i.e. the minimum number of crossings in any diagram of \(L\). Let \(\alpha_3(L)\) denote the three-page index of \(L\), meaning the minimal number of arcs needed to represent \(L\) in a three-page presentation. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the explicit inequality relating \(\alpha_3(L)\) and \(c(L)\) under these hypotheses?

## Choices
A. \[\alpha_3(L)\le 3c(L)-1.\]
B. \[\alpha_3(L)\le 3c(L)-2.\]
C. \[\alpha_3(L)=3c(L)-1.\]
D. \[\alpha_3(L)\le 3c(L).\]
E. \[\alpha_3(L)\le 3c(L)-1\quad\text{for every non-split, nontrivial link }L.\]

## Question
Let \(N\ge 2\), \(b>0\), and let \(q\) satisfy \(1+\frac{2b}{N-1}<q<q^e\), where \(q^e\) is the admissible upper exponent for the problem. Let \(H^2_{rd}:=\{f\in H^2(\mathbb R^N): f(x)=f(|x|)\}\) be the radial subspace of \(H^2(\mathbb R^N)\), write \(\|v\|=\|v\|_{L^2(\mathbb R^N)}\) and \(\|\Delta v\|=\|\Delta v\|_{L^2(\mathbb R^N)}\), and let
\[
D:=\frac{N(q-1)-2b}{4},\qquad E:=q+1-D.
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the uniform weighted Gagliardo--Nirenberg inequality for all \(v\in H^2_{rd}\)?

## Choices
A. There exists a constant \(C>0\) such that for every \(v\in H^2_{rd}\),
\[
\int_{\mathbb R^N} |v(x)|^{1+q}|x|^b\,dx \le C\,\|v\|^E\,\|\Delta v\|^D.
\]
B. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
C. For every \(v\in H^2_{rd}\) there exists a constant \(C_v>0\) such that
\[
\int_{\mathbb R^N} |v(x)|^{1+q}|x|^b\,dx \le C_v\,\|v\|^E\,\|\Delta v\|^D.
\]
D. There exists a sharp constant \(\texttt{C}_{opt}>0\) such that for every \(v\in H^2_{rd}\),
\[
\int_{\mathbb R^N} |v(x)|^{1+q}|x|^b\,dx \le \texttt{C}_{opt}\,\|v\|^{D}\,\|\Delta v\|^{E}.
\]
E. There exists a sharp constant \(\texttt{C}_{opt}>0\) such that for every \(v\in H^2_{rd}\),
\[
\int_{\mathbb R^N} |v(x)|^{1+q}|x|^b\,dx \le \texttt{C}_{opt}\,\|v\|^E\,\|\Delta v\|^D,
\]
and the same conclusion remains valid at the endpoint \(q=1+\frac{2b}{N-1}\).
