## Question
Let \(r\in\{2,3,4\}\), let \(\underline a=(a_1,\ldots,a_n)\in [r-1]^n\), and write \(V_q^{\underline a}=\bigotimes_{j=1}^n V_q(\omega_{a_j})\), where \(V_q(\omega_{a_j})\) is the irreducible \(U_q(\mathfrak{sl}_r)\)-module of fundamental highest weight \(\omega_{a_j}\). Let \(\mathcal W_{\underline a}\) be the fully reduced web basis of \(\mathsf{Inv}(V_q^{\underline a})\). Suppose \([n]=I_1\sqcup\cdots\sqcup I_m\) is a partition into intervals, giving a clasp sequence \(\underline C=(\underline c_1,\ldots,\underline c_m)\) with \(\underline c_i=(a_j)_{j\in I_i}\) and associated weights \(\lambda_i=\operatorname{wt}(\underline c_i)=\sum_{j\in I_i}\omega_{a_j}\in\Lambda^+\), where \(\Lambda^+\) is the set of dominant integral weights. Let \(\pi_{\underline C}\) denote the clasping map associated to \(\underline C\). For an element \([W]_q\in \mathcal W_{\underline a}\), which of the following statements is equivalent to both \([W]_q\notin \ker \pi_{\underline C}\) and the condition that \(W\) has no trips that start and end in the same clasp?

## Choices
A. For each \(i\in[m]\) and every \(\underline c_i\)-cut path \(\gamma\), it is not the case that \(\operatorname{weight}(\gamma)\) is strictly smaller than \(\operatorname{weight}(\underline c_i)\) in the dominance ordering on \(\Lambda^+\).
B. \(W\) is non-convex with respect to \(\underline C\), meaning that for every \(i\in[m]\) and every \(\underline c_i\)-cut path \(\gamma\), if \(\operatorname{weight}(\gamma)=\sum_{k=1}^{r-1} b_k\omega_k\) and \(\operatorname{weight}(\underline c_i)=\sum_{k=1}^{r-1} c_k\omega_k\), then \(b_k\ge c_k\) for all \(k\).
C. \(W\) is non-convex with respect to \(\underline C\), meaning that for every clasp \(\underline c_i\), every cut path \(\gamma\) whose endpoints lie on vertices of that clasp satisfies \(\operatorname{weight}(\gamma)\ge \operatorname{weight}(\underline c_i)\) in the dominance ordering on \(\Lambda^+\).
D. \(W\) is non-convex with respect to \(\underline C\), meaning that for every \(i\in[m]\) and every \(\underline c_i\)-cut path \(\gamma\), one has \(\operatorname{weight}(\gamma)>\operatorname{weight}(\underline c_i)\) in the dominance ordering on \(\Lambda^+\).
E. \(W\) is non-convex with respect to \(\underline C\), meaning that for every \(i\in[m]\) and every \(\underline c_i\)-cut path \(\gamma\), one has \(\operatorname{weight}(\gamma)\ge \operatorname{weight}(\underline c_i)\) in the dominance ordering on \(\Lambda^+\).

## Question
Fix a probability space \((S,\Delta,\mathbb P)\) and an ideal \(\mathcal I\) on \(\mathbb N\). Let \((U,d)\) be a metric space, let \(\{x_n\}_{n\in\mathbb N}\subset U\), and let \(\{X_n\}_{n\in\mathbb N}\) be the associated constant \(U\)-valued random variables, i.e. \(X_n(s)=x_n\) for all \(s\in S\). Suppose there exist \(r\ge 0\) and \(x_*\in U\) such that \(\{x_n\}_{n\in\mathbb N}\) is \(r\)-\(\mathcal I\) convergent to \(x_*\), meaning that for every \(\varepsilon>0\), \(\{n\in\mathbb N: d(x_n,x_*)\ge r+\varepsilon\}\in\mathcal I\). Here \(X_n\xrightarrow[r]{\mathcal I^{\mathbb P}} X_*\) means that for every \(\varepsilon>0\), \(\{n\in\mathbb N: \rho(X_n,X_*)\ge r+\varepsilon\}\in\mathcal I\), where \(\rho(X,Y)=\inf\{\eta>0:\mathbb P(d(X,Y)>\eta)\le \eta\}\) is the Ky Fan metric. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence of a random-variable limit under these assumptions?

## Choices
A. There exists a random variable \(X_*\) such that \(\mathbb P(X_*=x_*)=1\) and, for every \(\varepsilon>0\), \[\{n\in\mathbb N: \rho(X_n,X_*)\ge r+\varepsilon\}\subseteq \{n\in\mathbb N: d(x_n,x_*)\ge r+\varepsilon\}.\]
B. There exists a random variable \(X_*\) such that \(\mathbb P(X_*=x_*)=1\) and there is a single set \(A\in\mathcal I\) for which \[\mathbb P(d(X_n,X_*)>r+\varepsilon)=0\quad\text{for every }\varepsilon>0\text{ and every }n\notin A.\]
C. There exists a random variable \(X_*\) such that \(\mathbb P(X_*=x_*)=1\) and, for every \(\varepsilon>0\), \[\{n\in\mathbb N: \rho(X_n,X_*)\ge r+\varepsilon\}=\{n\in\mathbb N: d(x_n,x_*)\ge r+\varepsilon\}\in\mathcal I.\]
D. There exists a random variable \(X_*\) such that \(\mathbb P(X_*=x_*)=1\) and the same conclusion \(X_n\xrightarrow[r]{\mathcal I^{\mathbb P}}X_*\) would still follow if the \(X_n\) were replaced by arbitrary \(U\)-valued random variables satisfying only \(\mathbb P(X_n=x_n)\to 1\).
E. There exists a random variable \(X_*\) such that \(\mathbb P(X_*=x_*)=1\) and \(X_n\xrightarrow[r]{\mathcal I^{\mathbb P}} X_*\).

## Question
Fix an integer $k\ge 3$. For $N\ge 1$, write $[N]:=\{1,2,\dots,N\}$, and define
\[
f_k(N):=\max\left\{\sum_{a\in A}\frac1a:\ A\subseteq [N]\ \text{is LCM-$k$-free}\right\},
\]
where $A$ is called LCM-$k$-free if there do not exist distinct $a_1,\dots,a_k\in A$ such that all pairwise least common multiples are equal, i.e.
\[
\operatorname{lcm}(a_i,a_j)=\operatorname{lcm}(a_1,a_2)\qquad(1\le i<j\le k).
\]
Define
\[
c_k:=\frac{k-2}{e\,((k-2)!)^{1/(k-2)}}.
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the asymptotic lower bound for $f_k(N)$ as $N\to\infty$?

## Choices
A. \[
f_k(N)\ge (\log N)^{\frac{k-1}{e\,((k-1)!)^{1/(k-1)}}-o(1)}\qquad (N\to\infty).
\]
B. \[
f_k(N)\ge (\log N)^{c_k-o(1)}\qquad (N\to\infty),
\]
for some positive constant $c_k$ depending only on $k$.
C. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
D. \[
f_k(N)\ge (\log N)^{c_k-o(1)}
\]
for every sufficiently large $N$, where now $c_k=\dfrac{k-2}{((k-2)!)^{1/(k-2)}}$.
E. \[
f_k(N)\ge (\log N)^{c_k+o(1)}\qquad (N\to\infty),
\]
with $c_k=\dfrac{k-2}{e\,((k-2)!)^{1/(k-2)}}$.

\[

\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the quantitative local H\"older estimate for every $0<\gamma<1$ and every Kor\'anyi ball $B_{2R}(\xi_0)\Subset\Omega$ with $0<R<1$?

## Choices

\le \frac{c}{R^{\gamma}}\left(\|u\|_{L^\infty(B_R(\xi_0))}+\operatorname{Tail}_{p-1,sp,p}(u;\xi_0,R)\right).
\]
B. For every $0<\gamma<1$ and every Kor\'anyi ball $B_{2R}(\xi_0)\Subset\Omega$ with $0<R<1$, there exists a finite constant $C=C(u,\xi_0,R,Q,p,s,\gamma)$ such that

\le \frac{c}{R^{\gamma}}\left(\|u\|_{L^\infty(B_R(\xi_0))}+\operatorname{Tail}_{p-1,sp,p}(u;\xi_0,R)\right).

\[

## Question
Let \((\alpha,\beta)\) be a minimally intersecting filling pair on a closed orientable surface \(S_2\) of genus 2; that is, \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) are homotopically distinct simple closed curves in minimal position, their complement \(S_2\setminus(\alpha\cup\beta)\) is a disjoint union of topological disks, and their geometric intersection number is minimal among filling pairs on \(S_2\). For such a pair on \(S_2\), the complement consists of two polygonal disk regions. If the type \(\{m,n\}\) records the unordered pair of side counts of these complementary regions, which types can occur?

## Choices

B. The only combinatorial restrictions are that \(m\) and \(n\) are even and that \(m+n=16\); hence the possible types are exactly \(\{4,12\}\), \(\{6,10\}\), and \(\{8,8\}\).
C. Exactly the types \(\{4,12\}\) and \(\{8,8\}\).
D. Exactly the types \(\{4,12\}\) and \(\{6,10\}\) can occur; the balanced type \(\{8,8\}\) cannot arise because the two complementary disks cannot be combinatorially identical.
E. The intermediate type \(\{6,10\}\) does not occur for a minimally intersecting filling pair on \(S_2\).

## Question
Let \(\mathbb R_+\) denote the positive real numbers. Let \(\mathcal M\subseteq \mathbb R^n\) be a nondegenerate submanifold, and let \(\psi_1,\dots,\psi_n:\mathbb R_+\to(0,1)\) be non-increasing functions. A point \(x=(x_1,\dots,x_n)\in\mathbb R^n\) is called \((\psi_1,\dots,\psi_n)\)-approximable if there exist infinitely many integers \(q\ge 1\) and integers \(p_1,\dots,p_n\) such that \(|qx_i-p_i|<\psi_i(q)\) for every \(i\). Which statement gives the correct criterion, in terms of \(\sum_{q=1}^\infty \psi_1(q)\cdots \psi_n(q)\), for whether almost all points of \(\mathcal M\) (with respect to the induced Lebesgue measure on \(\mathcal M\)) are \((\psi_1,\dots,\psi_n)\)-approximable?

## Choices
A. This zero-full law with the series \(\sum_{q=1}^\infty \psi_1(q)\cdots\psi_n(q)\) is valid only when \(\mathcal M\) is locally parametrized in Monge form; for an arbitrary nondegenerate submanifold, convergence of that series alone does not imply measure zero.
B. The set of \((\psi_1,\dots,\psi_n)\)-approximable points on \(\mathcal M\) has full measure if \(\sum_{q=1}^\infty \psi_1(q)\cdots\psi_n(q)\) diverges and, in addition, there exist \(s>0\) and infinitely many dyadic integers \(q=2^t\) such that \(q\,\psi_1(q)\cdots\psi_n(q)\ge q^{-s}\); if the series converges, then the set has measure zero.
C. The set of \((\psi_1,\dots,\psi_n)\)-approximable points on \(\mathcal M\) has full measure (with respect to the induced Lebesgue measure on \(\mathcal M\)) if \(\sum_{q=1}^\infty \psi_1(q)\cdots \psi_n(q)\) diverges, and has measure zero if that series converges.
D. The set of \((\psi_1,\dots,\psi_n)\)-approximable points on \(\mathcal M\) has full measure if \(\sum_{q=2}^\infty \psi_1(q)\cdots\psi_n(q)(\log q)^{n-1}\) diverges, and has measure zero if that series converges.
E. A sufficient condition is that \(\sum_{q=1}^\infty \psi_1(q)\cdots\psi_n(q)\) diverges: in that case the set of \((\psi_1,\dots,\psi_n)\)-approximable points on \(\mathcal M\) has full measure, without asserting the converse or the convergent case.

For a minimal model $X^{\min}$ of $X$, a finite quasi-\'etale cover means a finite morphism $X'\to X^{\min}$ that is \'etale in codimension one, and a $\mathbb{Q}$-factorialization of $X'$ means a small birational model $X''\to X'$ with $X''$ $\mathbb{Q}$-factorial. E. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

## Question
Fix primes \(p\) and \(\ell\) with \(p \equiv 1 \pmod 4\) and \(\ell \neq p\). Let \(X(\mathbf F_p)\) be the Paley graph on \(\mathbf F_p\) (with vertices \(\mathbf F_p\), and \(x\) adjacent to \(y\) when \(x-y\) is a nonzero square in \(\mathbf F_p\)). Consider connected graph covers of \(X(\mathbf F_p)\) with deck group \(\mathbf Z/\ell\) whose defining arc-labeling is invariant under the translation action of the additive group of \(\mathbf F_p\); these are called translation-invariant \(\mathbf Z/\ell\)-covers. Which statement correctly describes the role of the adjacency spectrum in classifying such covers?

## Choices
A. For any two translation-invariant \(\mathbf Z/\ell\)-covers \(X^{\alpha}\) and \(X^{\beta}\) of \(X(\mathbf F_p)\), if \(X^{\alpha} \cong X^{\beta}\) as graphs, then their adjacency spectra are equal as multisets.
B. For any two translation-invariant \(\mathbf Z/\ell\)-covers \(X^{\alpha}\) and \(X^{\beta}\) of \(X(\mathbf F_p)\), if their adjacency spectra are equal as multisets, then there is an isomorphism \(X^{\alpha} \cong X^{\beta}\) that commutes with the projections to \(X(\mathbf F_p)\) and with the \(\mathbf Z/\ell\)-deck actions.
C. For any two connected \(\mathbf Z/\ell\)-covers \(X^{\alpha}\) and \(X^{\beta}\) of \(X(\mathbf F_p)\), even without assuming translation invariance of the defining arc-labeling, equality of adjacency spectra as multisets implies \(X^{\alpha} \cong X^{\beta}\) as graphs.
D. For any two translation-invariant \(\mathbf Z/\ell\)-covers \(X^{\alpha}\) and \(X^{\beta}\) of \(X(\mathbf F_p)\), if their adjacency spectra are equal as multisets, then \(X^{\alpha} \cong X^{\beta}\) as graphs.
E. The same spectral rigidity statement remains valid with \(\mathbf F_p\) replaced by \(\mathbf F_q\) for every finite field \(\mathbf F_q\) with \(q \equiv 1 \pmod 4\): for any prime \(\ell \neq \operatorname{char}(\mathbf F_q)\), equal adjacency spectra of translation-invariant \(\mathbf Z/\ell\)-covers of \(X(\mathbf F_q)\) imply graph isomorphism.

## Question
Let \(n\ge 3\) and \(\Omega=B_R(0)\subset \mathbb{R}^n\) for some \(R>0\), where \(B_r(0)\) denotes the ball of radius \(r\) centered at the origin. Let \(p,q\in\mathbb{R}\) satisfy
\[
p<\frac{n-2}{n-1},\qquad q<\frac{n-2}{n-1},
\]
and define
\[
f(s)=(1+s)^{-p/2},\qquad g(s)=(1+s)^{-q/2}.
\]
Assume the initial data \(u_0,w_0\in W^{1,\infty}(\overline\Omega)\) are positive and radially symmetric. Consider the Neumann problem
\[
\begin{cases}
u_t = \Delta u-\nabla\!\cdot\!\big(u f(|\nabla v|^2)\nabla v\big), & x\in\Omega,\ t>0,\\
0=\Delta v-\mu_w+w,\quad \mu_w(t)=|\Omega|^{-1}\int_\Omega w(x,t)\,dx, & x\in\Omega,\ t>0,\\
w_t = \Delta w-\nabla\!\cdot\!\big(w g(|\nabla z|^2)\nabla z\big), & x\in\Omega,\ t>0,\\
0=\Delta z-\mu_u+u,\quad \mu_u(t)=|\Omega|^{-1}\int_\Omega u(x,t)\,dx, & x\in\Omega,\ t>0,\\
\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial \nu}=\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial \nu}=\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial \nu}=\dfrac{\partial z}{\partial \nu}=0, & x\in\partial\Omega,\ t>0,\\
u(x,0)=u_0(x),\quad w(x,0)=w_0(x), & x\in\Omega,
\end{cases}
\]
where \(\nu\) on \(\partial\Omega\) denotes the outward unit normal. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence behavior of solutions to this system under these assumptions?

## Choices
A. There exist constants \(m_1,m_2>0\) such that, if the positive radially symmetric initial data additionally satisfy
\[
\int_{\Omega}u_0\,dx\ge m_1,
\qquad
\int_{\Omega}w_0\,dx\ge m_2,
\]
then the corresponding solution of the above system blows up in finite time.
B. There exist functions \(M_1,M_2\in C^0([0,R])\) such that, if the positive radially symmetric initial data additionally satisfy
\[
\int_{B_r(0)}u_0\,dx\ge M_1(r),\qquad \int_{B_r(0)}w_0\,dx\ge M_2(r)
\quad\text{for every } r\in(0,R),
\]
then the corresponding solution of the above system blows up in finite time.
C. There exist functions \(M_1,M_2\in C^0([0,R])\) such that, if the positive radially symmetric initial data additionally satisfy
\[
\int_{B_r(0)}u_0\,dx\ge M_1(r),\qquad \int_{B_r(0)}w_0\,dx\ge M_2(r)
\quad\text{for every } r\in(0,R),
\]
then the corresponding solution of the above system cannot remain globally bounded for all times.
D. There exist functions \(M_1,M_2\in C^0([0,R])\) such that, if the positive radially symmetric initial data additionally satisfy
\[
\int_{B_r(0)}u_0\,dx\ge M_1(r),\qquad \int_{B_r(0)}w_0\,dx\ge M_2(r)
\quad\text{for every } r\in(0,R),
\]
then the corresponding solution of the above system exists globally for all \(t>0\), but

## Question

Call \(\lambda\) Latin if its boxes can be filled with positive integers so that each row of length \(r\) contains each of \(1,2,\dots,r\) exactly once, and no column contains a repeated entry. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about every such \(\lambda\)?

## Choices

D. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.

## Question
Let \(\Omega\subset \mathbb{R}^2\) be a smooth bounded convex domain with inradius \(\operatorname{inr}(\Omega)=r\) (the radius of the largest ball contained in \(\Omega\)) and diameter \(D(\Omega)=D\). \[
\begin{cases}
u_t-\Delta u=0, & (x,t)\in \Omega\times \mathbb{R}^+,\\
\partial_{\nu}u=\lambda g(u), & (x,t)\in \partial\Omega\times \mathbb{R}^+,\\
u(x,0)=u_0(x), & x\in \Omega,
\end{cases}
\]
where \(\partial_{\nu}u\) denotes the outward normal derivative on \(\partial\Omega\), and a "pattern" means a non-constant stable stationary solution of this problem. B. There exists a parameter \(\lambda>0\) such that the problem admits a pattern.

## Question
Let k\in\{2,3\} and let H\subseteq \binom{[n]}{k} be a shifted k-uniform hypergraph on [n]=\{1,\dots,n\}, meaning that whenever \{s_1<\cdots<s_k\}\in H and r_i\le s_i for all i, then \{r_1<\cdots<r_k\}\in H. Write
\[
M(H)=\{G\subseteq \tbinom{[n]}{k}:\ \Delta^e(G)=H\},
\]
where \Delta^e denotes exterior algebraic shifting, and call H matroidal if M(H) is the set of bases of a matroid. Which statement is equivalent to H being matroidal?

## Choices
A. For every \(i\in[n]\), the link \(\operatorname{lk}_H(i)=\{e\setminus\{i\}: i\in e\in H\}\subseteq \binom{[n]\setminus\{i\}}{k-1}\) is an initial lex-segment; equivalently, a vertexwise lex-initial condition already characterizes matroidality.
B. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
C. The hyperedges of H form an initial colex-segment; equivalently, there exists m such that H consists of exactly the first m elements of \binom{[n]}{k} in colexicographic order.
D. There exists a permutation \(\pi\in S_n\) such that \(\pi(H)=\{\pi(T):T\in H\}\) is an initial lex-segment of \(\binom{[n]}{k}\); equivalently, it is enough for H to be relabel-isomorphic to an initial lex-segment.
E. For each i\in[n], the subfamily \(\{e\setminus\{i\}: \min(e)=i,\ e\in H\}\subseteq \binom{\{i+1,\dots,n\}}{k-1}\) is an initial lex-segment; equivalently, within each block of hyperedges having the same smallest vertex, H is lex-initial.

## Question
Let $R$ be a $k$-algebra. For each positive integer $m$, write $\operatorname{Der}_k^m(R)$ for the set of $m$-integrable $k$-derivations of $R$, where a $k$-derivation $\delta$ is called $m$-integrable if it lies in the image of the truncation map $\operatorname{HS}_k^m(R)\to \operatorname{HS}_k^1(R)=\operatorname{Der}_k(R)$. Which statement correctly describes the eventual behavior of the family $\operatorname{Der}_k^m(R)$?

## Choices

There exists a positive integer \(M\), depending only on the base field \(k\) and not on \(R\), such that for every \(k\)-algebra \(R\) and every \(m\ge M\), \(\operatorname{Der}_k^m(R)=\operatorname{Ider}_k(R)\); equivalently, the family \(\operatorname{Der}_k^m(R)\) stabilizes to \(\operatorname{Ider}_k(R)\) with a uniform bound valid for all \(R\)."

## Question
Let \(\Sigma\subset \mathbb{S}^3\subset \mathbb{R}^4\) be a smooth embedded surface in the round 3-sphere, with genus at least \(1\). For \(v\in \mathring{B}^4=\{v\in\mathbb{R}^4:|v|<1\}\), let
\[
F_v(x)=(1-|v|^2)\frac{x-v}{|x-v|^2}-v
\]
be the associated conformal transformation of \(\mathbb{S}^3\), and set \(\Sigma_v:=F_v(\Sigma)\). If \(\mathbb{S}^3\setminus \Sigma=A\cup A^*\) is the decomposition into connected components, let \(A_v:=F_v(A)\), let \(d_v\) be the signed distance to \(\Sigma_v\) (positive on \(A_v\), negative on the other component), and define
\[
\Sigma_{(v,t)}=\partial\{x\mid d_v(x)<t\}, \qquad (v,t)\in\mathring{B}^4\times[-\pi,\pi].
\]
Thus \(\{\Sigma_{(v,t)}\}_{(v,t)\in \mathring{B}^4\times[-\pi,\pi]}\) is the canonical five-parameter family of \(\Sigma\). Which quantitative area estimate holds for this family, and in which case does equality occur?

## Choices
A. \[\max_{(v,t)\in \mathring{B}^4\times[-\pi,\pi]} \operatorname{Area}(\Sigma_{(v,t)})\ge 2\pi^2,\] and equality holds if and only if \(\Sigma\) is the Clifford torus.
B. \[\max_{(v,t)\in \mathring{B}^4\times[-\pi,\pi]} \operatorname{Area}(\Sigma_{(v,t)})\ge 2\pi^2.\]
C. \[\max_{(v,t)\in \mathring{B}^4\times[-\pi,\pi]} \operatorname{Area}(\Sigma_{(v,t)})\ge 2\pi^2,\] and equality holds whenever \(\Sigma\) is conformally equivalent to the Clifford torus.
D. \[\max_{v\in \mathring{B}^4} \operatorname{Area}(\Sigma_v)\ge 2\pi^2,\] and equality holds if and only if \(\Sigma\) is the Clifford torus.
E. \[\max_{(v,t)\in \mathring{B}^4\times[-\pi,\pi]} \operatorname{Area}(\Sigma_{(v,t)})\le 2\pi^2,\] and equality holds if and only if \(\Sigma\) is the Clifford torus.

H(z,t):=t^p+a(z)t^q \qquad (t\ge 0).
\]
Assume \(\mathcal A:\Omega_T\times \mathbb R^{Nn}\to \mathbb R^{Nn}\) is a Carathéodory vector field such that for some constants \(0<\nu\le L<\infty\),
\[
\mathcal A(z,\xi)\cdot \xi\ge \nu\big(|\xi|^p+a(z)|\xi|^q\big),
\qquad
|\mathcal A(z,\xi)|\le L\big(|\xi|^{p-1}+a(z)|\xi|^{q-1}\big)
\]
for a.e. \(z\in\Omega_T\) and all \(\xi\in\mathbb R^{Nn}\). Let \(Q_\rho(z_0)\) denote a parabolic cylinder of radius \(\rho\) centered at \(z_0\). Suppose
\[
 u\in C(0,T;L^2(\Omega,\mathbb R^N))\cap L^q(0,T;W^{1,q}(\Omega,\mathbb R^N))\cap L^\infty(\Omega_T)
\]
is a weak solution in \(\Omega_T\) of
\[
 u_t-\operatorname{div}\mathcal A(z,\nabla u)=-\operatorname{div}\big(|F|^{p-2}F+a(z)|F|^{q-2}F\big).
\]

For $\pi\in\Pi_\psi$, let $\bar{\mathcal N}(\pi)$ denote the set of stable nilpotent orbits in $\mathfrak h(\overline F)$ attached to $\pi$. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the dimensions of the orbits in $\bar{\mathcal N}(\pi)$ under these hypotheses?

so the maximal dimension occurring in $\bar{\mathcal N}(\pi)$ is always the dimension of $d_{H^\vee}(\mathrm{Ad}(H^\vee)N_\phi)$.

There is a constant \(k_S = k_S(\chi(S)) \ge 1\), depending only on \(\chi(S)\), and a constant \(k_{M\backslash\!\!\backslash S} \ge 1\), depending only on \(\chi(S)\) and \(\mathfrak c(M\backslash\!\!\backslash S)\), so that the following inequalities hold: \(d_{\mathcal C(S)}(c^s,c^u) \le k_S\,\mathrm{vol}(M)+k_{M\backslash\!\!\backslash S}\), and \(d_{\mathcal C(S)}(c^s,c^u) \le k_S\,\ell_M(\gamma)+k_{M\backslash\!\!\backslash S}\) for every closed geodesic \(\gamma\subset M\), whether or not \(\gamma\) intersects \(S\) essentially.

There is a constant \(k_S = k_S(\chi(S)) \ge 1\), depending only on \(\chi(S)\), and a constant \(k_{M\backslash\!\!\backslash S} \ge 1\), depending only on \(\mathfrak c(M\backslash\!\!\backslash S)\), so that the following inequalities hold: \(d_{\mathcal C(S)}(c^s,c^u) \le k_S\,\mathrm{vol}(M)+k_{M\backslash\!\!\backslash S}\), and \(d_{\mathcal C(S)}(c^s,c^u) \le k_S\,\ell_M(\gamma)+k_{M\backslash\!\!\backslash S}\) whenever \(\gamma\) is a closed geodesic that intersects \(S\) essentially.

## Question
Let $d\in\{2,3\}$ and consider the compressible elastodynamics Cauchy problem on $\mathbb{R}^d\times[0,\infty)$
\[
\begin{cases}
\partial_t\rho+\operatorname{div}(\rho u)=0,\\
\partial_t(\rho u)+\operatorname{div}(\rho u\otimes u)+\nabla P(\rho)=\operatorname{div}(\rho F F^\top),\\
\partial_t F+u\cdot\nabla F=\nabla u\,F,\\
(\rho,u,F)|_{t=0}=(\rho_0,u_0,F_0),
\end{cases}
\]
where $P(\rho)=\rho^\gamma$ with $\gamma>1$, $I$ is the identity matrix, and $H_x^k=H^k(\mathbb{R}^d)$, $L_x^2=L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)$. Assume
\[
(\rho_0-1,\,u_0-x,\,F_0-I)\in H_x^3(\mathbb{R}^d),
\]
and that for some diffeomorphism $\xi_0:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}^d$ and every $x\in\mathbb{R}^d$,
\[
\rho_0(x)\det F_0(x)=1,\qquad F_0(x)=\nabla_y\xi_0(y),\qquad y=\xi_0^{-1}(x),
\]
together with
\[
\|\rho_0-1\|_{H_x^3}+\|u_0-x\|_{H_x^3}+\|F_0-I\|_{H_x^3}+\|\xi_0^{-1}-x\|_{H_x^4}\le \varepsilon.
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the uniform global existence, uniqueness, and decay of solutions $(\rho,u,F)$ under these assumptions?

## Choices
A. There exists a small constant $\varepsilon>0$ such that, for every initial datum $(\rho_0,u_0,F_0)$ satisfying
\[
(\rho_0-1,\,u_0-x,\,F_0-I)\in H_x^3(\mathbb{R}^d),\qquad \rho_0(x)\det F_0(x)=1\ \text{for all }x\in\mathbb{R}^d,
\]
\[
\operatorname{div}(\rho_0 F_0^{\top})=0,
\]
and
\[
\|\rho_0-1\|_{H_x^3}+\|u_0-x\|_{H_x^3}+\|F_0-I\|_{H_x^3}\le \varepsilon,
\]
the Cauchy problem admits a unique global strong solution $(\rho,u,F)$ with
\[
\Big(\rho(x,t)-\frac{1}{(1+t)^d},\;u(x,t)-\frac{x}{1+t},\;F(x,t)-(1+t)I\Big)\in C\big([0,\infty);H_x^3(\mathbb{R}^d)\big),
\]

## Question
For each positive integer $n$, let $[n]=\{1,2,\ldots,n\}$. The empty word and every one-letter word are both Andr\'e I and Andr\'e II. For $n\ge 2$, if a permutation $\sigma$ of $[n]$ is written as $\sigma=\tau\,\min(\sigma)\,\tau'$, then $\sigma$ is an Andr\'e I permutation (respectively, an Andr\'e II permutation) if both $\tau$ and $\tau'$ are Andr\'e I (respectively, Andr\'e II) and the maximum (respectively, minimum) letter of the subword $\tau\tau'$ lies in $\tau'$. Let $\AndI_n$ and $\AndII_n$ denote the sets of Andr\'e I and Andr\'e II permutations of $[n]$, respectively. Let $\RS_n$ denote the set of simsun permutations of $[n]$, where a permutation $\sigma=\sigma_1\cdots\sigma_n$ is simsun if $\sigma_n=n$, it has no double descents, and this remains true after successively removing $n,n-1,\ldots,1$; here a double descent is an index $i$ with $\sigma_i>\sigma_{i+1}>\sigma_{i+2}$. For a permutation $\sigma$, write $\des(\sigma)$ for its number of descents, $\ides(\sigma)=\des(\sigma^{-1})$ for its number of inverse descents, and $\maj(\sigma)$ for its major index (the sum of its descent positions). Which statement holds for every $n$?

## Choices
A. The bivariate statistic $(\ides,\des)$ is equidistributed on $\AndI_n$, $\AndII_n$, and $\RS_n$; equivalently,
$$
\sum_{\sigma\in\AndI_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)}
=
\sum_{\sigma\in\AndII_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)}
=
\sum_{\sigma\in\RS_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)}.
$$
B. The joint distribution of $(\ides,\des,\maj)$ is the same on $\AndI_n$, $\AndII_n$, and $\RS_n$ after interchanging descents and inverse descents on the simsun side; equivalently,
$$
\sum_{\sigma\in\AndI_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}
=
\sum_{\sigma\in\AndII_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}
=
\sum_{\sigma\in\RS_n} s^{\des(\sigma)} t^{\ides(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}.
$$
C. The joint distribution of $(\ides,\des,\maj)$ is the same on $\AndI_n$ and $\AndII_n$, and moreover the pair $(\ides,\des)$ is equidistributed with $\RS_n$; equivalently,
$$
\sum_{\sigma\in\AndI_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}
=
\sum_{\sigma\in\AndII_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)},
\qquad
\sum_{\sigma\in\AndII_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)}
=
\sum_{\sigma\in\RS_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)}.
$$
D. The joint distribution of $(\ides,\des,\maj)$ is the same on $\AndI_n$, $\AndII_n$, and $\RS_n$ for each fixed descent set; equivalently, for every subset $D\subseteq [n-1]$,
$$
\sum_{\substack{\sigma\in\AndI_n\\ \Des(\sigma)=D}} s^{\ides(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}
=
\sum_{\substack{\sigma\in\AndII_n\\ \Des(\sigma)=D}} s^{\ides(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}
=
\sum_{\substack{\sigma\in\RS_n\\ \Des(\sigma)=D}} s^{\ides(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}.
$$
E. The joint distribution of $(\ides,\des,\maj)$ is the same on $\AndI_n$, $\AndII_n$, and $\RS_n$; equivalently,
$$
\sum_{\sigma\in\AndI_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}
=
\sum_{\sigma\in\AndII_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}
=
\sum_{\sigma\in\RS_n} s^{\ides(\sigma)} t^{\des(\sigma)} q^{\maj(\sigma)}.
$$

Let $\mu$ be a translation-invariant, ergodic, finite-energy edge percolation measure on $\mathbb{Z}^2$. C. Almost surely, exactly one of the following two alternatives occurs: $\mu_{\hp}$ has a unique infinite cluster while $\mu^*_{\hp}$ has no infinite cluster; or $\mu^*_{\hp}$ has a unique infinite cluster while $\mu_{\hp}$ has no infinite cluster.
D. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
E. Almost surely, at most one of $\mu_{\hp}$ and $\mu^*_{\hp}$ has an infinite cluster; equivalently, simultaneous primal and dual percolation in the upper half-plane cannot occur.

Let \((M_i,g_i)\) be Riemannian manifolds of dimensions \(m_i\) for \(i=1,2\), let \(g=g_1\times g_2\), and write \(g_+^t:=g_1\times t^2g_2\) for the right anisotropic \(t\)-homothety on \(M_1\times M_2\). Suppose that for \(t\le 1\) one has
\[
\mathcal H_{g_+^t}^{m_1+m_2-1}(\Sigma)\le t^{m_2-1}\,\mathcal H_g^{m_1+m_2-1}(\Sigma).
\]
Which of the following statements is equivalent to equality in this estimate?

## Choices

B. If one writes the \(g\)-unit normal as \(\nu=a\nu_1+b\nu_2\) with \(a^2+b^2=1\), then equality is equivalent to the identity \(t^2a^2+b^2=1\) holding at every regular point of a dense subset of \(\Sigma\).
C. At \(\mathcal H_g^{m_1+m_2-1}\)-a.e. regular point \((x,y)\in\Sigma\), the approximate tangent hyperplane \(T_{(x,y)}\Sigma\) contains the whole subspace \(T_yM_2\).
D. At \(\mathcal H_g^{m_1+m_2-1}\)-a.e. regular point of \(\Sigma\), the \(M_1\)-component of the \(g\)-unit normal vanishes.
E. At \(\mathcal H_g^{m_1+m_2-1}\)-a.e. regular point of \(\Sigma\), the \(g\)-unit normal makes a constant angle with the \(M_2\)-factor.

## Question
Fix a depth parameter \(\delta>0\), and let \(T^\delta\) be the singular integral operator
\[
(T^\delta f)(x)=\frac{1}{2\delta}\,\mathrm{P.V.}\!\int_{\mathbb R}\coth\!\left(\frac{\pi (y-x)}{2\delta}\right)f(y)\,dy.
\]
Let \(n\in\mathbb N\) with \(n\ge 1\), let \(\mathbf c=(c_1,\dots,c_n)\) satisfy \(0<c_1<\cdots<c_n\), let \(\mathbf x=(x_1,\dots,x_n)\in\mathbb R^n\), and let \(U^{(n)}(\cdot,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf x)\) be the corresponding \(n\)-soliton solution of
\[
u_t+\frac{1}{\delta}u_x+2uu_x+T^\delta u_{xx}=0
\quad\text{on }\mathbb R\times\mathbb R.
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the uniform orbital stability in \(H^{n/2}(\mathbb R)\) of perturbations of \(U^{(n)}(0,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf x)\)?

## Choices
A. For every \(\epsilon>0\), there exists \(\delta_0>0\) such that for every initial datum \(u_0\in H^{n/2}(\mathbb R)\) with \(\|u_0-U^{(n)}(0,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf x)\|_{H^{n/2}}<\delta_0\), if \(u(t)\) is the corresponding solution, then for every \(t\in\mathbb R\),
\[
\inf_{\tau\in\mathbb R,\ \mathbf y\in\mathbb R^n}\|u(t)-U^{(n)}(\tau,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf y)\|_{H^{n/2}}<\epsilon.
\]
B. For every \(\epsilon>0\), there exists \(\delta_0>0\) such that for every initial datum \(u_0\in H^{n/2}(\mathbb R)\) with \(\|u_0-U^{(n)}(0,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf x)\|_{H^{n/2}}<\delta_0\), if \(u(t)\) is the corresponding solution, then for every \(t\in\mathbb R\),
\[
\inf_{\mathbf y\in\mathbb R^n}\|u(t)-U^{(n)}(t,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf y)\|_{H^{n/2}}<\epsilon.
\]
C. For every \(\epsilon>0\), there exists \(\delta_0>0\), depending only on \(\epsilon\), \(n\), and the fixed depth parameter \(\delta\), such that for every choice of wave speeds \(0<c_1<\cdots<c_n\), every \(\mathbf x\in\mathbb R^n\), and every initial datum \(u_0\in H^{n/2}(\mathbb R)\) with \(\|u_0-U^{(n)}(0,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf x)\|_{H^{n/2}}<\delta_0\), if \(u(t)\) is the corresponding solution, then for every \(t\in\mathbb R\),
\[
\inf_{\tau\in\mathbb R,\ \mathbf y\in\mathbb R^n}\|u(t)-U^{(n)}(\tau,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf y)\|_{H^{n/2}}<\epsilon.
\]
D. For every \(\epsilon>0\), there exists \(\delta_0>0\) such that for every initial datum \(u_0\in H^{n/2}(\mathbb R)\) with \(\|u_0-U^{(n)}(0,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf x)\|_{H^{n/2}}<\delta_0\), if \(u(t)\) is the corresponding solution, then for every \(t\in\mathbb R\),
\[
\inf_{\tau\in\mathbb R,\ a\in\mathbb R}\|u(t)-U^{(n)}(\tau,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf x+a(1,\dots,1))\|_{H^{n/2}}<\epsilon.
\]
E. For every \(\epsilon>0\), there exists \(\delta_0>0\) such that for every initial datum \(u_0\in H^{n/2}(\mathbb R)\) with \(\|u_0-U^{(n)}(0,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf x)\|_{H^{n/2}}<\delta_0\), if \(u(t)\) is the corresponding solution, then there exists a single vector \(\mathbf y\in\mathbb R^n\), independent of \(t\), such that for every \(t\in\mathbb R\),
\[
\inf_{\tau\in\mathbb R}\|u(t)-U^{(n)}(\tau,\cdot;\mathbf c,\mathbf y)\|_{H^{n/2}}<\epsilon.
\]

## Question
Let $\mathbb{N}$ denote the positive integers, let $k\in\mathbb{N}$, and let $c:\mathbb{N}\to\{\text{red},\text{blue}\}$ be any 2-coloring of $\mathbb{N}$. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about the existence of $x,y\in\mathbb{N}$ whose values under $c$ make the relevant multiplicative and additive configurations monochromatic?

## Choices
A. There exist $x,y\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x$, $y$, $xy$, and every number of the form $x+iy$ with $1\le i\le k+1$ all have the same color under $c$; equivalently, the set $\{x,y,xy,x+iy: i\le k+1\}$ is monochromatic.
B. The same conclusion in fact holds for every finite coloring of $\mathbb{N}$, not just for 2-colorings: for any finite coloring of $\mathbb{N}$ there exist $x,y\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x$, $y$, $xy$, and every number of the form $x+iy$ with $1\le i\le k$ all have the same color.
C. There exist $x,y\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x$, $y$, $xy$, and every number of the form $x+iy$ with $1\le i\le k$ all have the same color under $c$; equivalently, the set $\{x,y,xy,x+iy: i\le k\}$ is monochromatic.
D. There exists a single $y\in\mathbb{N}$, depending only on $k$, such that for every 2-coloring $c:\mathbb{N}\to\{\mathrm{red},\mathrm{blue}\}$ there exists $x\in\mathbb{N}$ with $x$, $y$, $xy$, and every number of the form $x+iy$ with $1\le i\le k$ all having the same color under $c$.
E. There exist $x,y\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $x$, $y$, and $xy$ all have the same color under $c$; equivalently, the set $\{x,y,xy\}$ is monochromatic.

## Question
Let \(E\) be a holomorphic vector bundle of rank \(r\) over a compact K\"ahler manifold \(X\). Write \(S^kE\) for the \(k\)-th symmetric power of \(E\) and \(\det E\) for its determinant line bundle. Assume that \(E\) is uniformly weakly RC-positive, meaning the uniform version of weak RC-positivity: for some Hermitian metric on \(E\), the induced metric on the tautological line bundle \(O_{P(E^*)}(1)\) over the projectivized bundle \(P(E^*)\) has curvature positive on every fiber and at least \(r\) positive eigenvalues at every point. Here, a holomorphic vector bundle \(F\) is called uniformly RC-positive if for every point \(x\in X\) there exists a nonzero tangent vector \(u\in T_xX\) such that the Chern curvature satisfies \(R^F(u,\bar u,v,\bar v)>0\) for every nonzero fiber vector \(v\in F_x\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved about \(S^kE\otimes \det E\) and \(S^kE\) under these assumptions?

## Choices
A. For every integer \(k\ge 0\), the bundle \(S^kE\otimes \det E\) is uniformly RC-positive, and \(S^kE\) itself is uniformly RC-positive for all sufficiently large integers \(k\).
B. For every integer \(k\ge 0\), the bundle \(S^kE\otimes \det E\) is uniformly RC-positive, and \(S^{k+r}E\) is uniformly RC-positive for every integer \(k\ge 0\).
C. For every integer \(k\ge 0\), the bundle \(S^kE\otimes (\det E)^{-1}\) is uniformly RC-positive, and \(S^kE\) itself is uniformly RC-positive for all sufficiently large integers \(k\).
D. For every integer \(k\ge 0\), the bundle \(S^kE\otimes \det E\) is uniformly RC-positive.
E. For every integer \(k\ge 0\), the bundle \(S^kE\) is uniformly RC-positive, and \(S^kE\otimes \det E\) is uniformly RC-positive for all sufficiently large integers \(k\).

## Question
For each integer $n \ge 10$, let $X_n=2\mathbb{CP}^2\# n\overline{\mathbb{CP}^2}$, where $\#$ denotes connected sum and $\overline{\mathbb{CP}^2}$ is $\mathbb{CP}^2$ with the opposite orientation. Let $T(X_n)$ denote the Torelli group of $X_n$, i.e. the subgroup of the mapping class group (smooth isotopy classes of diffeomorphisms of $X_n$) consisting of classes represented by diffeomorphisms continuously isotopic to the identity. What is the strongest statement that can be proved about homomorphisms from $T(X_n)$ to $\mathbb{Z}^\infty$?

## Choices
A. For every $n\ge 10$, there exists a surjective homomorphism $\Phi:T(X_n)\to \mathbb{Z}^\infty$, where $\mathbb{Z}^\infty$ is the direct sum of countably infinitely many copies of $\mathbb{Z}$.
B. For every $n\ge 10$, there exists a homomorphism $\Phi:T(X_n)\to \mathbb{Z}^\infty$ whose image has infinite rank, but which need not be surjective onto $\mathbb{Z}^\infty$.
C. For every $n\ge 10$, every diffeomorphism of $X_n$ that is continuously isotopic to the identity determines, via the families Seiberg--Witten construction, a surjective homomorphism $T(X_n)\to \mathbb{Z}^\infty$.
D. There exists a single surjective homomorphism $\Phi:T(X_n)\to \mathbb{Z}^\infty$ for all sufficiently large $n$, but not necessarily for every $n\ge 10$.
E. For every $n\ge 10$, there exists a homomorphism $\Phi:T(X_n)\to \mathbb{Z}^\infty$ with infinite image.

## Question
Let \(\kappa \ge 3\) be a cardinal. Here a real tree is a geodesic metric space in which any two points are connected by a unique arc, and the valence of a point is the number of connected components of the complement of that point. The universal real tree of valence \(\kappa\) is the unique complete real tree in which every point has valence \(\kappa\). What is the strongest statement that can be proved for every such \(\kappa\) about a transfinite family of incomplete homogeneous real trees of valence \(\kappa\), indexed by the countable ordinals \(1 \le \alpha < \omega_1\), together with isometric embeddings between earlier and later members of the family?

## Choices
A. There exists a family \(\{T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}:1\le \alpha<\omega_1\}\) of incomplete homogeneous real trees of valence \(\kappa\) and isometric embeddings \(\psi_{\alpha,\beta}:T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}\hookrightarrow T_{\kappa}^{[\beta]}\) for all \(\alpha<\beta<\omega_1\) such that distinct countable ordinals give non-isometric trees, the embeddings are coherent in the sense that \(\psi_{\alpha,\gamma}=\psi_{\beta,\gamma}\circ\psi_{\alpha,\beta}\) whenever \(\alpha<\beta<\gamma\), and the direct union of this system is incomplete while its metric completion is isometric to the universal real tree of valence \(\kappa\).
B. There exists a family \(\{T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}:1\le \alpha<\omega_1\}\) of incomplete homogeneous real trees of valence \(\kappa\) and isometric embeddings \(\psi_{\alpha,\beta}:T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}\hookrightarrow T_{\kappa}^{[\beta]}\) for all \(\alpha<\beta<\omega_1\) such that distinct countable ordinals give non-isometric trees, the embeddings are coherent in the sense that \(\psi_{\alpha,\gamma}=\psi_{\beta,\gamma}\circ\psi_{\alpha,\beta}\) whenever \(\alpha<\beta<\gamma\), and for every countable limit ordinal \(\lambda<\omega_1\) the direct union \(\bigcup_{\alpha<\lambda}T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}\) is isometric to \(T_{\kappa}^{[\lambda]}\).
C. There exists a family \(\{T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}:1\le \alpha<\omega_1\}\) of incomplete homogeneous real trees of valence \(\kappa\) and isometric embeddings \(\psi_{\alpha,\beta}:T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}\hookrightarrow T_{\kappa}^{[\beta]}\) for all \(\alpha<\beta<\omega_1\) such that the embeddings are coherent in the sense that \(\psi_{\alpha,\gamma}=\psi_{\beta,\gamma}\circ\psi_{\alpha,\beta}\) whenever \(\alpha<\beta<\gamma\), and the direct union of this system is isometric to the universal real tree of valence \(\kappa\).
D. There exists a family \(\{T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}:1\le \alpha<\omega_1\}\) of incomplete homogeneous real trees of valence \(\kappa\) and isometric embeddings \(\psi_{\alpha,\beta}:T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}\hookrightarrow T_{\kappa}^{[\beta]}\) for all \(\alpha<\beta<\omega_1\) such that distinct countable ordinals give non-isometric trees, the embeddings are coherent in the sense that \(\psi_{\alpha,\gamma}=\psi_{\beta,gamma}\circ\psi_{\alpha,\beta}\) whenever \(\alpha<\beta<\gamma\), and the direct union of the subsystem indexed only by successor ordinals is already isometric to the universal real tree of valence \(\kappa\).
E. There exists a family \(\{T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}:1\le \alpha<\omega_1\}\) of incomplete homogeneous real trees of valence \(\kappa\) and isometric embeddings \(\psi_{\alpha,\beta}:T_{\kappa}^{[\alpha]}\hookrightarrow T_{\kappa}^{[\beta]}\) for all \(\alpha<\beta<\omega_1\) such that distinct countable ordinals give non-isometric trees, the embeddings are coherent in the sense that \(\psi_{\alpha,\gamma}=\psi_{\beta,\gamma}\circ\psi_{\alpha,\beta}\) whenever \(\alpha<\beta<\gamma\), and the direct union of this system is isometric to the universal real tree of valence \(\kappa\).

## Question
Let \((\mathcal M_1,g_1)\) and \((\mathcal M_2,g_2)\) be two smooth connected \(n\)-dimensional Riemannian manifolds with boundary. Let \(\omega_1\subset \mathcal M_1\) and \(\omega_2\subset \mathcal M_2\) be open connected subsets. For each \(j=1,2\), let \(L^2\Omega^1(\omega_j)\) denote the space of square-integrable 1-forms on \(\omega_j)\), and define the local source-to-final value map
\[
\mathcal N_j:\mathbb B_\delta\to L^2\Omega^1(\omega_j),\qquad \mathcal N_j(\mathbf f)=\mathbf u_{\mathbf f}(T,\cdot)|_{\omega_j},
\]
where \(\mathbb B_\delta\) is a small ball of admissible source 1-forms, and \(\mathbf u_{\mathbf f}\) is the corresponding solution of the geometric Navier--Stokes system on \([0,T]\times \mathcal M_j\) with zero initial and boundary data, evaluated at the final time \(T\). Assume there exists a diffeomorphism \(\Phi:\omega_1\to\omega_2\) such that, with \(\Phi^*\) denoting pullback on the relevant source and final-value 1-forms,
\[
\mathcal N_1\,\Phi^*=\Phi^*\,\mathcal N_2.
\]
What is the strongest statement that can be proved about \((\mathcal M_1,g_1)\) and \((\mathcal M_2,g_2)\) under these assumptions?

## Choices
A. The diffeomorphism \(\Phi\) extends to a global isometry \(\widetilde\Phi:\mathcal M_1\to\mathcal M_2\), and after identifying the manifolds by \(\widetilde\Phi\) the full nonlinear source-to-final value maps agree for all admissible \(L^2\)-source 1-forms, not merely for sources in the small ball \(\mathbb B_\delta\).
B. The intertwining identity implies equality, via \(\Phi\), of the localized spectral projections for the stationary Stokes operator on \(\omega_1\) and \(\omega_2\); however, this determines at most the domains of influence of the observation sets up to time \(T\), not the full manifolds up to isometry.
C. The intertwining identity already forces the first-order linearizations of \(\mathcal N_1\) and \(\mathcal N_2\) at \(0\) to agree under \(\Phi\); equivalently, the local source-to-final maps for the corresponding linearized Stokes systems coincide on the observation regions, which is weaker than global isometry.
D. There exists a sufficiently small \(\delta>0\), depending only on \(T\), such that intertwining on \(\mathbb B_\delta\) identifies the linearized Stokes dynamics on \(\omega_1\) and \(\omega_2\); to deduce that \((\mathcal M_1,g_1)\) and \((\mathcal M_2,g_2)\) are globally isometric one would need the additional hypothesis that \(T\) exceed the relevant geometric controllability time.
E. One of the remaining options is correct, but a stronger result can be proven.
